description:
bd mol run is a convenience combo (pour + update + pin) that feels like orchestration but lives in the data layer. This blurs the bd/gt architectural boundary.

## Current State
- bd mol run = bd pour + bd update --status=in_progress + bd pin --for me
- gt spawn depends on bd mol run (shells out to it)

## Proposed Change
Once gt spawn inlines the pour+assign+pin logic:
1. Deprecate bd mol run with a warning pointing to gt spawn
2. Eventually remove bd mol run entirely

## Why
- bd should be pure data operations (pour, update, pin, close)
- gt should own orchestration (spawn, sling, hook, handoff)
- Standalone bd users can run the three commands separately or script them

## Blocked By
gastown issue gt-r7jj2 (Move mol run semantics from bd into gt spawn)
title: Deprecate bd mol run after gt absorbs its semantics

description:
ROOT CAUSE: server_export_import_auto.go line 221 uses ResolveCollisions: true for ALL auto-imports. This is wrong.

Problem:
- ResolveCollisions is for branch merges (different issues with same ID)
- Auto-import should UPDATE existing issues, not create duplicates
- Every git pull creates NEW duplicate issues with different IDs
- Two agents ping-pong creating endless duplicates

Evidence:
- 31 duplicate groups found (bd duplicates)
- bd-236-246 are duplicates of bd-224-235
- Both agents keep pulling and creating more duplicates
- JSONL file grows endlessly with duplicates

The Fix:
Change checkAndAutoImportIfStale in server_export_import_auto.go:
- Remove ResolveCollisions: true (line 221)
- Use normal import logic that updates existing issues by ID
- Only use ResolveCollisions for explicit bd import --resolve-collisions

Impact: Critical - makes beads unusable for multi-agent workflows
title: Fix auto-import creating duplicates instead of updating issues
Session ended: gt-beads-crew-emma

description:
## Problem

bd cleanup deletes closed issues (converting them to tombstones) but does NOT prune expired tombstones. Users expect 'cleanup' to do comprehensive cleanup.

## Current Behavior

1. bd cleanup --force converts closed issues to tombstones
2. Expired tombstones (>30 days) remain in issues.jsonl  
3. User must separately run bd compact to prune tombstones
4. bd doctor warns about expired tombstones: Run bd compact to prune

## Expected Behavior

bd cleanup should also prune expired tombstones from issues.jsonl.

## Impact

With v0.30.0 making tombstones the default migration path, this UX gap becomes more visible. Users cleaning up their database should not need to know about a separate bd compact command.

## Proposed Solution

Call pruneExpiredTombstones() at the end of the cleanup command (same function used by compact).

## Files to Modify

- cmd/bd/cleanup.go - Add call to pruneExpiredTombstones after deleteBatch

## Acceptance Criteria

- bd cleanup --force prunes expired tombstones after deleting closed issues
- bd cleanup --dry-run shows what tombstones would be pruned
- JSON output includes tombstone prune results
title: bd cleanup should also prune expired tombstones
Test child issue

description:
After git pull updates .beads/issues.jsonl, daemon doesn't automatically re-import changes, causing stale data to be shown until next sync cycle (up to 5 minutes).

Reproduction:
1. Repo A: Close issues, export, commit, push
2. Repo B: git pull (successfully updates .beads/issues.jsonl)
3. bd show <issue> shows OLD status from daemon's SQLite db
4. JSONL on disk has correct new status

Root cause: Daemon sync cycle runs on timer (5min). When user manually runs git pull, daemon doesn't detect JSONL was updated externally and continues serving stale data from SQLite.

Impact:
- High for AI agents using beads in git workflows
- Breaks fundamental git-as-source-of-truth model
- Confusing UX: git log shows commit, bd shows old state
- Data consistency issues between JSONL and daemon

See WYVERN_SYNC_ISSUE.md for full analysis.
notes:
**Fixed in v0.21.2!**

The daemon auto-import is fully implemented:
- internal/autoimport package handles staleness detection
- internal/importer package provides shared import logic (used by both CLI and daemon)
- daemon's checkAndAutoImportIfStale() calls autoimport.AutoImportIfNewer()
- importFunc uses importer.ImportIssues() with auto-rename enabled
- All tests passing

The critical data corruption bug is FIXED:
✅ After git pull, daemon detects JSONL is newer (mtime check)
✅ Daemon auto-imports before serving requests
✅ No stale data served
✅ No data loss in multi-agent workflows

Verification needed: Run two-repo test to confirm end-to-end behavior.
title: Daemon fails to auto-import after git pull updates JSONL

description: Fix GitHub issue #202. The JSON output from bd show and bd list commands should include the dependency type field (and optionally created_at, created_by) to match internal storage format and enable better tooling integration.
notes:
PR #203 updated with cleaner implementation: https://github.com/steveyegge/beads/pull/203

## Final Implementation

Cleanest possible approach - no internal helper methods needed:

**Design:**
- `GetDependenciesWithMetadata()` / `GetDependentsWithMetadata()` - canonical implementations with full SQL query
- `GetDependencies()` / `GetDependents()` - thin wrappers that strip metadata for backward compat
- `scanIssuesWithDependencyType()` - shared helper for scanning rows with dependency type

**Benefits:**
- Single source of truth - the `...WithMetadata()` methods ARE the implementation
- Eliminated ~139 lines of duplicated SQL and scanning code
- All tests passing (14 dependency-related tests)
- Backward compatible
- dependency_type field appears correctly in JSON output

**Note on scan helpers:**
The duplication between `scanIssues()` and `scanIssuesWithDependencyType()` is necessary because they handle different SQL result shapes (16 vs 17 columns). This is justified as they serve fundamentally different purposes based on query structure.
title: bd show --json doesn't include dependency type field

description:
During JSONL migration (beads.jsonl → issues.jsonl), the git history backfill mechanism causes data loss by finding issues in the old beads.jsonl git history and incorrectly treating them as deleted.

A --no-git-history flag for 'bd import' and 'bd sync' would allow users to disable the git history fallback when it's causing problems.

Use cases:
- JSONL filename migrations
- Repos with complex git history
- Debugging import issues
- Performance (skip slow git scans)

Related: bd-0gh (migration causes spurious deletions)
title: Need --no-git-history flag to disable git history backfill during import

description:

Expected: Issues should only be updated when explicitly modified.

- False conflicts in git
- Confusing git history

Likely culprit: Daemon auto-import/export cycle or database migration touching all issues.
notes: Investigated thoroughly - unable to reproduce. The import logic has IssueDataChanged() checks before calling UpdateIssue (importer/importer.go:458). All tests pass. description:
bd doctor should detect old beads integration patterns and recommend migrating to bd prime approach.

## Current behavior
- bd doctor checks if Claude hooks are installed globally
- Doesn't check project-level integration (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md)
- Doesn't recommend migration for repos using old patterns

## Desired behavior
bd doctor should detect and suggest:

1. **Old slash command pattern detected**
   - Check for /beads:* references in AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md
   - Suggest: These slash commands are deprecated, use bd prime hooks instead
   
2. **No agent documentation**
   - Check if AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md exists
   - Suggest: Run 'bd onboard' or 'bd setup claude' to document workflow
   
3. **Old MCP-only pattern**
   - Check for instructions to use MCP tools but no bd prime hooks
   - Suggest: Add bd prime hooks for better token efficiency

4. **Migration path**
   - Show: 'Run bd setup claude to add SessionStart/PreCompact hooks'
   - Show: 'Update AGENTS.md to reference bd prime instead of slash commands'

## Example output

⚠ Warning: Old beads integration detected in CLAUDE.md
  Found: /beads:* slash command references (deprecated)
  Recommend: Migrate to bd prime hooks for better token efficiency
  Fix: Run 'bd setup claude' and update CLAUDE.md

💡 Tip: bd prime + hooks reduces token usage by 80-99% vs slash commands
  MCP mode: ~50 tokens vs ~10.5k for full MCP scan
  CLI mode: ~1-2k tokens with automatic context recovery

## Benefits
- Helps existing repos adopt new best practices
- Clear migration path for users
- Better token efficiency messaging
title: bd doctor should recommend bd prime migration for existing repos

description:
dispatched_by: gastown/crew/max

## Problem

When using `bd delete --hard --force <bead-id>`, the bead is not truly deleted. Instead, a tombstone record is created (status='tombstone', deleted_at=X).

This tombstone:
- Is invisible to `bd show` (returns 'not found')
- Cannot be reopened with `bd reopen` (returns 'not found')
- BUT blocks `bd create --id=<same-id>` with UNIQUE constraint error

## Reproduction

```bash
bd create --id=bd-test-123 'Test bead'
bd delete --hard --force bd-test-123
bd show bd-test-123           # 'not found' (expected)
bd reopen bd-test-123         # 'not found' (unexpected?)
bd create --id=bd-test-123 'New bead'  # UNIQUE constraint failed!
```

## Impact

Gas Town polecats that are removed and later respawned with the same name fail on agent bead creation. The workaround (PR #452 in gastown) uses `bd close` instead of `bd delete --hard --force`.

## Expected Behavior

Either:
1. `bd delete --hard --force` should truly delete the record (no tombstone), OR
2. Tombstones should be visible and reopenable, OR
3. `bd create` should handle tombstones gracefully (delete tombstone and create fresh)

## Discovered

While investigating polecat respawn failures in Gas Town. See gastown PR #452 for the workaround.
title: bd delete --hard --force creates tombstones that block bd create

description:
## Problem
`bd sync` leaves orphaned merge artifact files (beads.base.jsonl, beads.left.jsonl) after completion, causing:
1. Doctor warnings about 'Multiple JSONL files found'
2. Confusion during debugging
3. Potential stale data issues on next sync

## Root Cause
`SnapshotManager` creates these files for 3-way merge deletion tracking but `Cleanup()` is never called after sync completes (success or failure).

## Fix
Call `SnapshotManager.Cleanup()` at end of successful sync:

```go
// sync.go after successful validation
sm := NewSnapshotManager(jsonlPath)
sm.Cleanup()
```

## Files
- cmd/bd/sync.go (add cleanup call)
- cmd/bd/snapshot_manager.go (Cleanup method exists at line 188)
title: Sync should cleanup snapshot files after completion

description:
bd doctor incorrectly flags hash-based IDs as sequential when they are short (3-4 chars) and all-numeric (e.g., pf-158).

Root cause: isHashID() in cmd/bd/migrate_hash_ids.go:328-358 uses faulty heuristic:
- For IDs < 5 chars, only returns true if contains letters
- But base36 hash IDs can be 3+ chars and all-numeric (MinLength: 3)
- Example: pf-158 is valid hash ID but flagged as sequential

Fix: Check multiple IDs (10-20 samples) instead of single-ID pattern matching:
- Sample IDs across database 
- Check majority pattern (sequential vs hash format)
- Sequential: 1-4 digits (bd-1, bd-2...)
- Hash: 3-8 chars base36 (pf-158, pf-3s9...)

Impact: False positive warnings in bd doctor output
title: Fix bd doctor hash ID detection for short all-numeric hashes

description:
## Summary

Extend the existing `gt escalate` system with tiered routing, structured decision patterns, and integration with gates and patrol lifecycles.

## Background

From gate design discussion and workflow analysis. Gas Town needs:
1. Structured way for workers to request decisions (not just free-form escalation)
2. Tiered routing (Deacon -> Mayor -> Overseer) instead of direct-to-human
3. Integration with gates (timeout -> escalate)
4. Formalized patterns for all worker exit scenarios

## Current State (what exists)

The escalation system is already partially built:

- `gt escalate` command: CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM severity
- Creates bead tagged `escalation`
- Sends mail to `overseer`
- Mayor checks pending escalations on `gt prime`
- Exit type `ESCALATED` for polecats
- Event logging: `escalation_sent`

See: `gastown/docs/escalation.md`

## What's Missing

| Gap | Current | Proposed |
|-----|---------|----------|
| Tiered routing | Direct to overseer | Deacon -> Mayor -> Overseer |
| Decision pattern | Free-form topic | Structured options in issue |
| Categories | Just "escalation" tag | decision, help, blocked, failed, emergency |
| Gate integration | None | Gate timeout -> escalate |
| Patrol integration | Ad-hoc | Formalized paths |

## Design

### Escalation Categories

| Category | Description | Default Destination |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| `decision` | Multiple valid paths, need choice | Deacon -> Mayor |
| `help` | Need guidance/expertise | Deacon -> Mayor |
| `blocked` | Waiting on unresolvable dependency | Mayor |
| `failed` | Unexpected error, can't proceed | Deacon |
| `emergency` | Security/data integrity | Overseer (direct) |
| `gate_timeout` | Gate didn't resolve in time | Deacon |
| `lifecycle` | Worker stuck/needs recycle | Witness |

### Extended Command

```bash
# Current (unchanged)
gt escalate "Something went wrong"
gt escalate -s CRITICAL "Data corruption"

# New: Category-based
gt escalate --type decision "Which auth approach?"
gt escalate --type help "Need architecture guidance"
gt escalate --type blocked "Waiting on bd-xyz"
gt escalate --type failed "Tests failing unexpectedly"
gt escalate --type emergency "Security vulnerability found"

# New: Tiered routing
gt escalate --to deacon "Infra issue"
gt escalate --to mayor "Cross-rig coordination needed"
gt escalate --to overseer "Human judgment required"

# New: Structured decision
gt escalate --type decision \
  --question "Which authentication approach?" \
  --options "JWT tokens,Session cookies,OAuth2" \
  --context "Admin panel needs login" \
  --issue bd-xyz
```

### Decision Pattern in Issues

When `--type decision` is used, the issue is updated with structured format:

```markdown
## Decision Needed

**Question:** Which authentication approach?

| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| A | JWT tokens |
| B | Session cookies |
| C | OAuth2 |

**Context:** Admin panel needs login

**Escalated by:** beads/polecats/obsidian
**Escalated at:** 2026-01-01T15:00:00Z

**To resolve:**
1. Comment with chosen option (e.g., "Decision: A")
2. Reassign to work queue or original worker
```

This makes the issue the async communication channel for decisions.

### Tiered Escalation Flow

```
Worker encounters issue
    |
    v
gt escalate --type <category> --to <tier>
    |
    v
[Deacon receives]
    |
    +-- Can resolve? --> Updates issue, re-slings work
    |
    +-- Cannot resolve? --> gt escalate --to mayor --forward
                                |
                                v
                           [Mayor receives]
                                |
                                +-- Can resolve? --> Updates issue, re-slings
                                |
                                +-- Cannot resolve? --> gt escalate --to overseer
                                                            |
                                                            v
                                                       [Overseer resolves]
```

Each tier can resolve OR forward. The escalation bead tracks the chain via comments/notes.

### Integration Points

#### Gate Timeouts (bd-7zka.2)

```go
// In Witness patrol (timer gates)
if gate.Expired() {
    exec.Command("gt", "escalate",
        "--type", "gate_timeout",
        "--severity", "HIGH",
        "--issue", gate.BlockedIssueID,
        fmt.Sprintf("Gate %s timed out after %s", gate.ID, gate.Timeout)).Run()
}
```

#### Witness Patrol

Witness already detects stuck polecats. Formalize as escalation:

```go
// Instead of ad-hoc mail
exec.Command("gt", "escalate",
    "--type", "lifecycle",
    "--to", "mayor",
    "--issue", polecat.CurrentIssue,
    fmt.Sprintf("Polecat %s stuck: no progress for %d minutes", polecat.ID, minutes)).Run()
```

#### Refinery

On merge failures that can't be auto-resolved:

```go
exec.Command("gt", "escalate",
    "--type", "failed",
    "--issue", mr.IssueID,
    "Merge failed: "+reason).Run()
```

### Polecat Exit with Escalation

```bash
# Worker reaches decision point

# 1. Update issue with decision structure
bd update $ISSUE --notes "$(cat <<EOF
## Decision Needed

**Question:** Which approach for caching?

| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| A | Redis (external dependency) |
| B | In-memory (simpler, no persistence) |
| C | SQLite (local persistence) |

**Context:** API response times are slow, need caching layer.
EOF
)"

# 2. Escalate
gt escalate --type decision --issue $ISSUE "Caching approach needs decision"

# 3. Exit cleanly
gt done --exit ESCALATED
```

### New Issue Statuses

Consider adding to beads:

| Status | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| `decision_needed` | Waiting for decision from escalation |
| `escalated` | General escalation pending |

Or keep using `blocked` with escalation tag - simpler.

## Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Extend gt escalate
- Add `--type` flag for categories
- Add `--to` flag for routing (deacon, mayor, overseer)
- Add `--forward` flag for tier forwarding
- Keep backward compatibility

### Phase 2: Decision Pattern
- Add `--question`, `--options`, `--context` flags
- Auto-update issue with decision structure
- Parse decision from issue comments on resolution

### Phase 3: Gate Integration
- Add `gate_timeout` escalation type
- Witness: check timer gates, escalate on timeout
- Refinery: check GH gates, escalate on timeout/failure

### Phase 4: Patrol Integration
- Formalize Witness stuck-polecat as escalation
- Formalize Refinery merge-failure as escalation
- Unified escalation handling in Mayor

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `gt escalate --type decision` creates structured issue update
- [ ] `gt escalate --to deacon|mayor|overseer` routes correctly
- [ ] Gate timeouts trigger escalation (after bd-7zka.2)
- [ ] Witness uses escalation for stuck polecats
- [ ] Refinery uses escalation for merge failures
- [ ] Escalation chain tracked in bead (who forwarded to whom)
- [ ] `bd list --tag=escalation --type=decision` filters work

## References

- bd-7zka.2: Gate evaluation (uses escalation for timeouts)
- gastown/docs/escalation.md: Current escalation protocol

description:
Enable re-syncing from external systems (Jira, GitHub, Linear) by using external_ref as the primary matching key during imports. Currently imports treat any content change as a collision, making it impossible to sync updates from external systems without creating duplicates.

See GH #142 for detailed proposal and implementation plan.

Key changes needed:
1. Add findByExternalRef() query function
2. Update DetectCollisions() to match by external_ref first
3. Update import_shared.go to update existing issues when external_ref matches
4. Add index on external_ref for performance
5. Preserve local issues (no external_ref) from being overwritten

This enables hybrid workflows: import external backlog, break down with local tasks, re-sync anytime.

description:
The daemon fails to handle 'show' RPC commands when:
1) JSONL is newer than database (needs import)
2) git pull fails due to uncommitted changes

Symptoms:
- Daemon appears to run (ps shows process)
- 'bd list' and other commands work fine  
- 'bd show <id>' returns "failed to read response: EOF"
- No panic or error logged in daemon.log

Root cause likely: auto-import deadlock or state corruption when import is blocked by git conflicts.

Workaround: 
- Restart daemon after syncing git state (commit/push changes)
- OR use --no-daemon flag for all commands

The panic recovery added in server_lifecycle_conn.go:183 didn't catch any panics, confirming this isn't a panic-based crash.
notes: Root cause found and fixed: Two bugs - (1) nil pointer check missing in handleShow causing panic, (2) double JSON encoding in show.go ID resolution. Both fixed. bd show now works with daemon.
title: Daemon crashes silently on RPC query after startup

- **Test (Windows)**: ❌ FAILING (exit code 1)

All three failing jobs show identical pattern:
- Individual test output shows PASS for every test
- Final result: `FAIL github.com/steveyegge/beads/cmd/bd`
- Exit code 1 despite no visible test failures
- Last visible test output before failure: "No Reason Issue" test (TestCloseCommand/close_without_reason)

## Investigation So Far
1. All tests appear to pass when examined individually
2. Likely causes:
   - Race detector finding data races during test cleanup (`-race` flag)

2. Check for unreported test failures or panics
3. Examine test cleanup/teardown code
4. Review recent changes around close command tests
5. Consider if race detector is too sensitive or catching real issues

description:
## Context

Go binaries (including bd.exe) are commonly flagged by antivirus software as false positives due to heuristic detection. See docs/ANTIVIRUS.md for full details.

## Problem

Kaspersky and other AV software flag bd.exe as PDM:Trojan.Win32.Generic, causing it to be quarantined or deleted.

## Solution

Implement code signing for Windows releases using:
1. An EV (Extended Validation) code certificate
2. Integration with GoReleaser to sign Windows binaries during release

## Benefits

- Reduces false positive rates over time as the certificate builds reputation
- Provides tamper verification for users
- Improves SmartScreen trust rating on Windows
- Professional appearance for enterprise users

## Implementation Steps

1. Acquire EV code signing certificate (annual cost ~$300-500)
2. Set up signtool or osslsigncode in release pipeline
3. Update .goreleaser.yml to sign Windows binaries
4. Update checksums to include signed binary hashes
5. Document signing verification in ANTIVIRUS.md

## References

- docs/ANTIVIRUS.md - Current documentation
- bd-t4u1 - Original Kaspersky false positive report
- https://github.com/golang/go/issues/16292 - Go project discussion
title: Add Windows code signing for bd.exe releases

description:
The bd sync --import-only command incorrectly detects conflict markers when issue descriptions contain the text '<<<<<<<' or '>>>>>>>' as legitimate content (e.g., documentation about git conflict markers).

**Reproduction:**
1. Create issue with design field containing: 'Read file, extract <<<<<<< / ======= / >>>>>>> markers'
2. Export to JSONL (gets JSON-encoded as \u003c\u003c\u003c...)
3. Commit and push
4. Pull from remote
5. bd sync --import-only fails with: 'Git conflict markers detected in JSONL file'

**Root cause:**
The conflict detection appears to decode JSON before checking for conflict markers, causing false positives when issue content legitimately contains these strings.

**Expected behavior:**
Conflict detection should only trigger on actual git conflict markers (literal '<<<<<<<' bytes in the raw file), not on JSON-encoded content within issue fields.

**Test case:**
VC project at ~/src/dave/vc has vc-85 'JSONL Conflict Parser' which documents conflict parsing and triggers this bug.

**Suggested fixes:**
1. Only scan for literal '<<<<<<<' bytes (not decoded JSON content)
2. Parse JSONL first and only flag unparseable lines
3. Check git merge state (git status) to confirm actual conflict
4. Add --skip-conflict-check flag for override
title: bd sync false positive: conflict detection triggers on JSON-encoded angle brackets in issue content

description:
Create a marketplace for sharing molecule formulas using GitHub repos as the hosting backend.

## Architecture Update (Dec 2025)

**Formulas are the sharing layer.** With ephemeral protos (bd-rciw), the architecture is:

```
Formulas ──cook──→ [ephemeral proto] ──pour/wisp──→ Mol/Wisp
    ↑                                                    │
    └────────────────── distill ─────────────────────────┘
```

- **Formulas**: JSON source files (.formula.json) - the thing you share
- **Protos**: Transient compilation artifacts - auto-deleted after use
- **Mols/Wisps**: Execution instances - not shared directly

**Key operations:**
- `bd distill <mol-id>` → Extract formula from completed work
- `bd mol publish <formula>` → Share to GitHub
- `bd mol install <url>` → Fetch from GitHub
- `bd pour <formula>` → Cook and spawn (proto is ephemeral)

## Why GitHub?

GitHub solves multiple problems at once:
- **Hosting**: Raw file URLs for formula.json
- **Versioning**: Git tags (v1.0.0, v1.2.0)
- **Auth**: GitHub tokens for private formulas
- **Discovery**: GitHub search, topics, stars
- **Collaboration**: PRs for contributions, issues for bugs
- **Organizations**: Natural scoping (@anthropic/, @gastown/)

## URL Scheme

```bash
# Direct GitHub URL
bd mol install github.com/anthropics/mol-code-review

# With version tag
bd mol install github.com/anthropics/mol-code-review@v1.2.0

# Shorthand (via registry lookup)
bd mol install @anthropic/mol-code-review
```

## Architecture

Each formula lives in its own repo (like Go modules):
```
github.com/anthropics/mol-code-review/
├── formula.json      # The formula
├── README.md         # Documentation
└── CHANGELOG.md      # Version history
```

## ID Namespace

| Entity | ID Format | Example |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| Formula (GitHub) | `github.com/org/repo` | `github.com/anthropics/mol-code-review` |
| Installed formula | `mol-name` | `mol-code-review` |
| Poured instance | `<db>-mol-xxx` | `bd-mol-b8c` |
notes: Deferred - focusing on Christmas launch first
title: Mol Mall: Formula marketplace using GitHub as backend

description:
Extract a formula from completed work (mol, wisp, or epic).

**Key change**: Distill works on execution artifacts (mols/wisps/epics), not protos.
Protos are ephemeral - they don't persist. Distillation extracts patterns from
actual executed work.

## Usage
```bash
bd distill bd-mol-xyz -o my-workflow.formula.json
bd distill bd-epic-abc -o feature-workflow.formula.json
```

## Use Cases
- **Emergent patterns**: Structured work manually, want to templatize it
- **Modified execution**: Poured a formula, added custom steps, want to capture
- **Learning from success**: Extract what made a complex mol succeed

## Implementation
1. Load mol/wisp/epic subgraph (root + all children)
2. Convert to formula JSON structure
3. Extract variables from patterns (titles, descriptions)
4. Generate step IDs from issue titles (slugify)
5. Write .formula.json file

## Output Format
```json
{
  "formula": "my-workflow",
  "description": "...",
  "version": 1,
  "vars": { ... },
  "steps": [ ... ]
}
```

## Architecture Note
This closes the formula lifecycle loop:
  Formulas ──cook──→ Mols ──distill──→ Formulas

All sharing happens via formulas. Mols contain execution context and aren't shared.
title: bd distill: Extract formula from mol/epic

description:
Import a formula file to the local catalog (search path).

**Replaces**: "bd mol promote" (proto-to-proto concept is obsolete with ephemeral protos)

## Usage
```bash
# Add a formula file to project catalog
bd formula add my-workflow.formula.json

# Add to user-level catalog
bd formula add my-workflow.formula.json --scope user

# Add from URL
bd formula add https://example.com/workflow.formula.json
```

## Implementation
1. Parse the formula file (validate JSON structure)
2. Determine target directory based on scope:
   - project: .beads/formulas/
   - user: ~/.beads/formulas/
   - town: ~/gt/.beads/formulas/
3. Copy/download formula to target
4. Verify it is loadable: bd formula show <name>

## Flags
- `--scope <level>` - Where to add (project|user|town, default: project)
- `--name <name>` - Override formula name (default: from file)

## Note
This is for manually adding formulas. For GitHub-hosted formulas, use:
  bd mol install github.com/org/formula-name
title: bd formula add: Import formula to local catalog

description:
Download and cook a formula from a GitHub repository.

## Usage
```bash
# Direct GitHub URL
bd mol install github.com/anthropics/mol-code-review

# With version tag  
bd mol install github.com/anthropics/mol-code-review@v1.2.0

# Shorthand (future: via registry lookup)
bd mol install @anthropic/mol-code-review
```

## Implementation
1. Parse URL: extract org, repo, optional version tag
2. Construct raw URL: `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/org/repo/[tag]/formula.json`
3. Fetch formula.json via HTTP
4. Validate formula structure
5. Save to .beads/formulas/
6. Run cook to create local proto
7. Record in .beads/installed.json for update tracking

## installed.json Format
```json
{
  "mol-code-review": {
    "source": "github.com/anthropics/mol-code-review",
    "version": "v1.2.0",
    "installed_at": "2025-12-25T12:00:00Z"
  }
}
```

## Dependencies
- Requires network access
- Uses `gh` CLI or direct HTTP for auth (private repos)
title: bd mol install: Install formula from GitHub

description:
Check for newer versions of installed formulas and update them.

## Usage
```bash
bd mol update                    # Update all
bd mol update mol-code-review    # Update specific formula
bd mol update --check            # Just check, don't update
```

## Implementation
1. Read .beads/installed.json
2. For each installed formula:
   - Fetch latest tag from GitHub API
   - Compare with installed version
   - If newer: download, cook, update installed.json
3. Report what was updated

## Flags
- `--check` - Only check for updates, don't install
- `--force` - Reinstall even if up to date
- `--json` - Machine-readable output
title: bd mol update: Check and update installed formulas

description:
Search for formulas in the Mol Mall using GitHub's search API.

## Usage
```bash
bd mol search "code review"           # Free text search
bd mol search --topic molecule         # By GitHub topic
bd mol search --org anthropics         # By organization
bd mol search --stars ">10"            # By popularity
```

## Implementation
1. Use GitHub Search API: `/search/repositories`
2. Filter by topic:mol-formula or naming convention
3. Parse results, show name/description/stars/version
4. Support pagination for large result sets

## Output
```
NAME                              STARS  DESCRIPTION
github.com/anthropics/mol-code-review  42  AI-assisted code review workflow
github.com/gastown/mol-polecat-work    12  Standard polecat work lifecycle
```

## Discovery Convention
Repos should have:
- Topic: `mol-formula` or `beads-molecule`
- Name starting with `mol-`
- formula.json in root
title: bd mol search: Find formulas using GitHub API

description:
Publish a formula to a GitHub repository for sharing.

## Usage
```bash
# Create new repo and publish
bd mol publish mol-my-workflow --repo github.com/myorg/mol-my-workflow

# Update existing repo
bd mol publish mol-my-workflow --repo github.com/myorg/mol-my-workflow --tag v1.1.0
```

## Implementation
1. Export proto to formula.json (if not already a file)
2. Validate formula structure
3. Create GitHub repo if --create flag (uses `gh repo create`)
4. Copy formula.json to repo
5. Generate README.md from formula description
6. Commit and push
7. Create git tag if --tag specified
8. Add mol-formula topic to repo

## Flags
- `--repo <url>` - Target GitHub repo (required)
- `--tag <version>` - Create version tag
- `--create` - Create repo if doesn't exist
- `--private` - Create as private repo

## Workflow
```bash
# Full publish flow
bd mol distill my-epic --name my-workflow
bd mol promote bd-proto-xxx --as my-workflow
bd mol publish mol-my-workflow --repo github.com/me/mol-my-workflow --create --tag v1.0.0
```
title: bd mol publish: Push formula to GitHub repo

description:
Add versioning support to formulas for tracking updates.

## Formula Version Field
```json
{
  "formula": "mol-code-review",
  "version": "1.2.0",
  "min_bd_version": "0.25.0",
  ...
}
```

## Version Semantics
- Use semver: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH
- MAJOR: Breaking changes to step structure
- MINOR: New optional steps or variables
- PATCH: Documentation, bug fixes

## Git Tag Integration
- `bd mol install repo@v1.2.0` fetches that tag
- `bd mol install repo` fetches latest tag (or main if no tags)
- `bd mol publish --tag v1.2.0` creates tag

## Proto Version Tracking
Local proto should store:
```
source_repo: github.com/anthropics/mol-code-review
source_version: v1.2.0
installed_at: 2025-12-25T12:00:00Z
```

This enables `bd mol update` to check for newer versions.
title: Formula versioning: Version field and git tag integration

description:
Track installed formulas for update checking and provenance.

## File Format
```json
{
  "mol-code-review": {
    "source": "github.com/anthropics/mol-code-review",
    "version": "v1.2.0",
    "installed_at": "2025-12-25T12:00:00Z",
    "formula_hash": "abc123..."
  },
  "mol-polecat-work": {
    "source": "local",
    "version": "1",
    "installed_at": "2025-12-24T10:00:00Z"
  }
}
```

## Commands That Update This
- `bd mol install` - Adds entry
- `bd mol update` - Updates version/timestamp
- `bd cook` - Adds entry with source:local
- `bd mol uninstall` - Removes entry (new command)

## Usage
```bash
bd mol list --installed    # Shows installed with source/version
bd mol outdated            # Shows formulas with available updates
```

## Sync Behavior
- installed.json is gitignored (local state)
- Only formulas/ directory is synced
- Each clone tracks its own installations
title: Installation tracking: .beads/installed.json

description:
Observed in VC repo: database has 1137 issues but beads.jsonl only has 309. Running 'bd export -o .beads/issues.jsonl' doesn't update the file. Running 'bd import' hangs indefinitely.

Reproduction context:
- VC repo: 1137 issues in DB, 309 in JSONL
- Created 4 new issues with bd create
- bd export didn't write them to JSONL
- bd import hung (possibly daemon lock conflict?)

Need to investigate:
1. Why export doesn't update JSONL when DB has more issues
2. Why import hangs
3. Daemon lock interaction with export/import
4. File path handling (issues.jsonl vs beads.jsonl)
notes:
## Root Cause Analysis

The issue was NOT about file path handling or export failing to update JSONL. The actual problem was:

**Import/Export hanging when daemon is running** due to SQLite lock contention:

1. When daemon is connected, `PersistentPreRun` in main.go returns early without initializing the `store` variable
2. Import/Export commands then tried to open the database directly with `sqlite.New(dbPath)` 
3. This blocked waiting for the database lock that the daemon already holds → **HANGS INDEFINITELY**

## Solution Implemented

Modified both import.go and export.go to:
1. Detect when `daemonClient` is connected
2. Explicitly close the daemon connection before opening direct SQLite access
3. Added debug logging to help diagnose similar issues

This ensures import/export commands always run in direct mode, avoiding lock contention.

## File Path Handling

The file path confusion (issues.jsonl vs beads.jsonl) was a red herring. The code uses `filepath.Glob("*.jsonl")` which correctly finds ANY `.jsonl` file in `.beads/` directory, so both filenames work.
title: Investigate bd export/import sync issue - database and JSONL out of sync

description: Move migrateDirtyIssuesTable, migrateExternalRefColumn, migrateCompositeIndexes, migrateClosedAtConstraint, migrateCompactionColumns, migrateSnapshotsTable, migrateCompactionConfig, migrateCompactedAtCommitColumn, migrateExportHashesTable, migrateContentHashColumn to a separate migrations.go file

description: Replace 5-second polling sync loop with event-driven architecture that reacts instantly to changes. Eliminates stale data issues while reducing CPU ~60%. - Skip redundant imports (mtime check prevents self-trigger loops)
- Add server.Stop() in serverErrChan case (clean shutdown)
- Fallback ticker (60s) when watcher unavailable (ensures remote sync)

Ready to make default after integration test (bd-1f4086c5.1) passes.
title: Event-driven daemon architecture

2. SingleMutationLatency: measures latency from mutation to JSONL update
3. RapidMutationBatching: verifies multiple mutations batch into single export

Once event-driven mode is default, remove the t.Skip() line and the test will validate <500ms latency.
title: Integration test: mutation to export latency

description:
**Problem:**
When users run `bd compact --analyze` with daemon running, they get:
```
Error: compact requires SQLite storage
```

This is misleading because they ARE using SQLite (via daemon), but the command needs DIRECT SQLite access.

**Current behavior:**
- Error message suggests they don't have SQLite
- No hint about using --no-daemon flag
- Related to issue #349 item #1

**Proposed fix:**
1. Update error messages in cmd/bd/compact.go lines 106-114 (analyze) and 121-137 (apply)
2. Add explicit hint: "Use --no-daemon flag to bypass daemon"
3. Change SQLite check error from "requires SQLite storage" to "failed to open database in direct mode"

**Files to modify:**
- cmd/bd/compact.go (lines ~106-137)

**Testing:**
- Run with daemon: `bd compact --analyze` should show clear error + hint
- Run with --no-daemon: `bd compact --analyze --no-daemon` should work
- Verify error message is actionable
title: Fix compact --analyze/--apply error messages to clarify direct mode requirement
Blocking issue

description:
# Problem

The cmd/bd test suite is painfully slow:
- **279 tests** in cmd/bd alone
- Full suite takes **8+ minutes** to run
- Even with the 16 slowest integration tests now tagged with `integration` build tag, the remaining tests still take forever

This makes the development loop unusable. We can't wait 8+ minutes every time we want to run tests.

# Root Cause Analysis

## 1. Sheer Volume
279 tests is too many for a single package. Even at 0.1s per test, that's 28 seconds minimum just for cmd/bd.

## 2. Each Test Creates Full Database + Temp Directories
Every test does heavy setup:
- Creates temp directory (`t.TempDir()` or `os.MkdirTemp`)
- Initializes SQLite database
- Sets up git repo in many cases
- Creates full storage layer

Example from the tests:
```go
func setupCLITestDB(t *testing.T) string {
    tmpDir := createTempDirWithCleanup(t)
    runBDInProcess(t, tmpDir, "init", "--prefix", "test", "--quiet")
    return tmpDir
}
```

This happens 279 times!

## 3. Tests Are Not Properly Categorized
We have three types of tests mixed together:
- **Unit tests** - should be fast, test single functions
- **Integration tests** - test full workflows, need DB/git
- **End-to-end tests** - test entire CLI commands

They're all lumped together in cmd/bd, all running every time.

# What We've Already Fixed

Added `integration` build tags to 16 obviously-slow test files:
- import_profile_test.go (performance benchmarking tests)
- export_mtime_test.go (tests with time.Sleep calls)
- cli_fast_test.go (full CLI integration tests)
- delete_test.go, import_uncommitted_test.go, sync_local_only_test.go (git integration)
- And 10 more in internal/ packages

These are now excluded from the default `go test ./...` run.

# Proposed Solutions

## Option 1: Shared Test Fixtures (Quick Win)
Create a shared test database that multiple tests can use:
```go
var testDB *sqlite.SQLiteStorage
var testDBOnce sync.Once

func getSharedTestDB(t *testing.T) storage.Storage {
    testDBOnce.Do(func() {
        // Create one DB for all tests
    })
    return testDB
}
```

**Pros**: Easy to implement, immediate speedup
**Cons**: Tests become less isolated, harder to debug failures

## Option 2: Table-Driven Tests (Medium Win)
Collapse similar tests into table-driven tests:
```go
func TestCreate(t *testing.T) {
    tests := []struct{
        name string
        args []string
        want string
    }{
        {"basic issue", []string{"create", "Test"}, "created"},
        {"with description", []string{"create", "Test", "-d", "desc"}, "created"},
        // ... 50 more cases
    }
    
    db := setupOnce(t) // Setup once, not 50 times
    for _, tt := range tests {
        t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
            // test using shared db
        })
    }
}
```

**Pros**: Dramatically reduces setup overhead, tests run in parallel
**Cons**: Requires refactoring, tests share more state

## Option 3: Split cmd/bd Tests Into Packages (Big Win)
Move tests into focused packages:
- `cmd/bd/internal/clitests` - CLI integration tests (mark with integration tag)
- `cmd/bd/internal/unittests` - Fast unit tests
- Keep only essential tests in cmd/bd

**Pros**: Clean separation, easy to run just fast tests
**Cons**: Requires significant refactoring

## Option 4: Parallel Execution (Quick Win)
Add `t.Parallel()` to independent tests:
```go
func TestSomething(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel() // Run this test concurrently with others
    // ...
}
```

**Pros**: Easy to add, can cut time in half on multi-core machines
**Cons**: Doesn't reduce actual test work, just parallelizes it

## Option 5: In-Memory Databases (Medium Win)
Use `:memory:` SQLite databases instead of file-based:
```go
store, err := sqlite.New(ctx, ":memory:")
```

**Pros**: Faster than disk I/O, easier cleanup
**Cons**: Some tests need actual file-based DBs (export/import tests)

# Recommended Approach

**Short-term (this week)**:
1. Add `t.Parallel()` to all independent tests in cmd/bd
2. Use `:memory:` databases where possible
3. Create table-driven tests for similar test cases

**Medium-term (next sprint)**:
4. Split cmd/bd tests into focused packages
5. Mark more integration tests appropriately

**Long-term (backlog)**:
6. Consider shared test fixtures with proper isolation

# Current Status

We've tagged 16 files with `integration` build tag, but the remaining 279 tests in cmd/bd still take 8+ minutes. This issue tracks fixing the cmd/bd test performance specifically.

# Target

Get `go test ./...` (without `-short` or `-tags=integration`) down to **under 30 seconds**.


# THE REAL ROOT CAUSE (Updated Analysis)

After examining the actual test code, the problem is clear:

## Every Test Creates Its Own Database From Scratch

Look at `create_test.go`:
```go
func TestCreate_BasicIssue(t *testing.T) {
    tmpDir := t.TempDir()                          // ← Creates temp dir
    testDB := filepath.Join(tmpDir, ".beads", "beads.db")
    s := newTestStore(t, testDB)                   // ← Opens NEW SQLite connection
                                                    // ← Runs migrations
                                                    // ← Sets config
    // ... actual test (3 lines)
}

func TestCreate_WithDescription(t *testing.T) {
    tmpDir := t.TempDir()                          // ← Creates ANOTHER temp dir
    testDB := filepath.Join(tmpDir, ".beads", "beads.db")
    s := newTestStore(t, testDB)                   // ← Opens ANOTHER SQLite connection
    // ... actual test (3 lines)
}
```

**This happens 279 times!**

## These Tests Don't Need Isolation!

Most tests are just checking:
- "Can I create an issue with a title?"
- "Can I create an issue with a description?"
- "Can I add labels?"

They don't conflict with each other. They could all share ONE database!

## The Fix: Test Suites with Shared Setup

Instead of:
```go
func TestCreate_BasicIssue(t *testing.T) {
    s := newTestStore(t, t.TempDir()+"/db")  // ← Expensive!
    // test
}

func TestCreate_WithDesc(t *testing.T) {
    s := newTestStore(t, t.TempDir()+"/db")  // ← Expensive!
    // test
}
```

Do this:
```go
func TestCreate(t *testing.T) {
    // ONE setup for all subtests
    s := newTestStore(t, t.TempDir()+"/db")
    
    t.Run("basic_issue", func(t *testing.T) {
        t.Parallel()  // Can run concurrently - tests don't conflict
        // test using shared `s`
    })
    
    t.Run("with_description", func(t *testing.T) {
        t.Parallel()
        // test using shared `s`
    })
    
    // ... 50 more subtests, all using same DB
}
```

**Result**: 50 tests → 1 database setup instead of 50!

## Why This Works

SQLite is fine with concurrent reads and isolated transactions. These tests:
- ✅ Create different issues (no ID conflicts)
- ✅ Just read back what they created
- ✅ Don't depend on database state from other tests

They SHOULD share a database!

## Real Numbers

Current:
- 279 tests × (create dir + init SQLite + migrations) = **8 minutes**

After fix:
- 10 test suites × (create dir + init SQLite + migrations) = **30 seconds**
- 279 subtests running in parallel using those 10 DBs = **5 seconds**

**Total: ~35 seconds instead of 8 minutes!**

## Implementation Plan

1. **Group related tests** into suites (Create, List, Update, Delete, etc.)
2. **One setup per suite** instead of per test
3. **Use t.Run() for subtests** with t.Parallel()
4. **Keep tests that actually need isolation** separate (export/import tests, git operations)

This is way better than shuffling tests into folders!
notes:
## Progress Update (2025-11-21)

✅ **Completed**:
- Audited all 280 tests, created TEST_SUITE_AUDIT.md ([deleted:bd-c49])
- Refactored create_test.go to shared DB pattern ([deleted:bd-y6d])
- Proven the pattern works: 11 tests now run in 0.04s with 1 DB instead of 11

❌ **Current Reality**:
- Overall test suite: Still 8+ minutes (no meaningful change)
- Only 1 of 76 test files refactored
- Saved ~10 DB initializations out of 280

## Acceptance Criteria (REALISTIC)

This task is NOT complete until:
- [ ] All P1 files refactored (create ✅, dep, stale, comments, list, ready)
- [ ] Test suite runs in < 2 minutes
- [ ] Measured and verified actual speedup

## Next Steps

1. Refactor remaining 5 P1 files: dep_test.go, stale_test.go, comments_test.go, list_test.go, ready_test.go
2. Measure actual time improvement after each file
3. Continue with P2 files if needed to hit <2min target
title: cmd/bd test suite is absurdly slow - 279 tests taking 8+ minutes

attached_args: Refactor ComputeContentHash repetitive code

The ComputeContentHash() method in internal/types/types.go (lines 87-190) is over 100 lines of repetitive code:

```go
h.Write([]byte(i.Title))
h.Write([]byte{0}) // separator
h.Write([]byte(i.Description))
h.Write([]byte{0})
h.Write([]byte(i.Design))
// ... repeated 40+ times
```

Consider:
1. Using reflection to iterate over struct fields tagged for hashing
2. Creating a helper function that takes field name and value
3. Using a slice of fields to hash and iterating over it
4. At minimum, extracting the repetitive pattern into a helper

Location: internal/types/types.go:87-190

1. Check tool execution logs for full stack trace
2. Verify Git storage initialization at ~/.mcp_agent_mail_git_mailbox_repo
3. Review database setup (storage.sqlite3)

- Proof of concept for lightweight beads integration later

Note: The actual beads integration (bd-wfmw) will be lightweight HTTP client code only.

description:

description:
## Problem

When `--json` flag is specified, error output should return JSON format, not plain text. Currently only `bd show` uses `FatalErrorRespectJSON` - all other commands use direct `fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, ...)`.

## Audit Findings (bd-au0.7)

| File | stderr writes | Commands affected |
|------|---------------|-------------------|
| show.go | 51 | update, close, edit |
| dep.go | 41 | dep add/remove/tree/cycles |
| label.go | 19 | label add/remove/list |
| comments.go | ~10 | comments add/list |
| epic.go | ~5 | epic status/close-eligible |

## Solution

Replace `fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, ...) + os.Exit(1)` patterns with `FatalErrorRespectJSON()` in all commands that support `--json` flag.

## Pattern

```go
// Before (inconsistent)
fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "Error: %v\n", err)
os.Exit(1)

// After (respects --json)
FatalErrorRespectJSON("%v", err)
```

## Success Criteria

- All commands return JSON errors when `--json` flag is set
- Error format: `{"error": "message"}`
- Exit code 1 preserved for errors
title: Fix JSON error output consistency across all commands

description:
Test issues (bd-0do3, bd-cjxp, bd-phr2, etc.) keep appearing in ready/list output, cluttering real work. These appear to be leftover test data from test runs.

Need to investigate:
1. Why are test issues not being cleaned up after tests?
2. Are tests creating issues in the main database instead of test databases?
3. Should we add better test isolation or cleanup hooks?
4. Can we add a label/prefix to distinguish test issues from real issues?

These test issues have characteristics:
- Empty descriptions
- Generic titles like "Test issue 0", "Bug P0", "Issue to reopen with reason"
- Created around 2025-11-07 19:00-19:07
- Some assigned to test users like "alice", "bob", "testuser"
notes:
## Root Cause Analysis

**Problem**: Python MCP integration tests created test issues in production `.beads/beads.db` instead of isolated test databases.

**Evidence**:
- 29 test issues created on Nov 7, 2025 at 19:00-19:07
- Patterns: "Bug P0", "Test issue X", assignees "alice"/"bob"/"testuser"
- Git commit 0e8936b shows test issues committed to .beads/beads.jsonl
- Tests were being fixed for workspace isolation around the same time

**Why It Happened**:
1. Before commit 0e8936b, `test_client_lazy_initialization()` didn't set `BEADS_WORKING_DIR`
2. Tests fell back to discovering `.beads/` in the project root directory
3. Auto-sync committed test issues to production database

**Resolution**:
1. ✅ Closed 29 test pollution issues (bd-0do3, bd-cjxp, etc.)
2. ✅ Added `failIfProductionDatabase()` guard in Go test helpers
3. ✅ Added production pollution checks in RPC test setup
4. ✅ Created `conftest.py` with pytest safety checks for Python tests
5. ✅ Added `BEADS_TEST_MODE` env var to mark test execution
6. ✅ Tests now fail fast if they detect production database usage

**Prevention**:
- All test helper functions now verify database paths are in temp directories
- Python tests fail immediately if BEADS_DB points to production
- BEADS_TEST_MODE flag helps identify test vs production execution
- Clear error messages guide developers to use proper test isolation

**Files Modified**:
- cmd/bd/test_helpers_test.go - Added failIfProductionDatabase()
- internal/rpc/rpc_test.go - Added temp directory verification
- integrations/beads-mcp/tests/conftest.py - New file with pytest safeguards
title: Investigate why test issues persist in database

description:
Marshal and write errors silently ignored in writeResponse, can send partial JSON and hang clients.

Location: internal/rpc/server_lifecycle_conn.go:228-232

Problem:
- json.Marshal error ignored - cyclic reference sends corrupt JSON
- Write error ignored - connection closed, no indication to caller  
- WriteByte error ignored - client hangs waiting for newline
- Flush error ignored - partial data buffered

Current code:
func (s *Server) writeResponse(writer *bufio.Writer, resp Response) {
    data, _ := json.Marshal(resp)  // Ignored!
    _, _ = writer.Write(data)      // Ignored!
    _ = writer.WriteByte('\n')     // Ignored!
    _ = writer.Flush()             // Ignored!
}

Solution: Return errors, handle in caller, close connection on error

Impact: Client hangs waiting for response; corrupt JSON sent

Effort: 1 hour
title: Fix silent error handling in RPC response writing

description:
For consistency, bd pour and bd ephemeral should become bd mol pour and bd mol ephemeral:

Current:
  bd mol list                  # Available protos
  bd mol show <id>             # Proto details
  bd pour <proto>              # Create mol ← sticks out
  bd ephemeral <proto>         # Create ephemeral ← sticks out  
  bd mol bond <proto> <parent> # Attach to existing mol
  bd mol squash <id>           # Condense to digest
  bd mol burn <id>             # Discard

Proposed:
  bd mol list
  bd mol show <id>
  bd mol pour <proto>          # Moved under mol
  bd mol ephemeral <proto>     # Moved under mol
  bd mol bond <proto> <parent>
  bd mol squash <id>
  bd mol burn <id>

All molecule operations should be under bd mol for discoverability and consistency.
title: Move pour/ephemeral under bd mol subcommand

description:
The CompactedResult is a new response format that MCP clients need to understand, but it's not documented in CONTEXT_ENGINEERING.md.

## What's Missing
- Example CompactedResult JSON response
- How to detect if a result is compacted
- How to request full results (disable compaction or increase limit)
- Guidance on handling both list[IssueMinimal] and CompactedResult return types
- Migration guide for clients expecting list[Issue] from ready() and list()

## Documentation Gaps
Currently CONTEXT_ENGINEERING.md explains the optimization strategy but doesn't show:
1. Sample CompactedResult response
2. Client-side code examples for handling compaction
3. When compaction is triggered (>20 results)
4. How to get all results if needed

## Suggested Additions
Add new section \"Handling Large Result Sets\" with:
- CompactedResult schema documentation
- Python client example for handling both response types
- Guidance on re-issuing queries with better filters
title: Document CompactedResult response format in CONTEXT_ENGINEERING.md

description:
Add comprehensive tests for context cancellation and timeout behavior.

## Context
Part of context propagation work. Validates that bd-rtp and bd-yb8 work correctly.

## Test Coverage Needed

### 1. Cancellation Tests
- [ ] Import operation cancelled mid-stream
- [ ] Export operation cancelled mid-stream  
- [ ] Database query cancelled during long operation
- [ ] No corruption after cancellation
- [ ] Proper cleanup (defers execute, connections closed)

### 2. Timeout Tests
- [ ] Operations respect context deadlines
- [ ] Appropriate error messages on timeout
- [ ] State remains consistent after timeout

### 3. Signal Handling Tests
- [ ] SIGINT (Ctrl+C) triggers cancellation
- [ ] SIGTERM triggers graceful shutdown
- [ ] Multiple signals handled correctly

## Implementation Approach
```go
func TestImportCancellation(t *testing.T) {
    ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
    
    // Start import in goroutine
    go func() {
        err := runImport(ctx, largeFile)
        assert.Error(err, context.Canceled)
    }()
    
    // Cancel after short delay
    time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
    cancel()
    
    // Verify database integrity
    assertDatabaseConsistent(t, store)
}
```

## Files to Create/Update
- cmd/bd/import_test.go - cancellation tests
- cmd/bd/export_test.go - cancellation tests
- internal/storage/sqlite/*_test.go - context timeout tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All critical operations have cancellation tests
- [ ] Tests verify database integrity after cancellation
- [ ] Signal handling tested (if feasible)
- [ ] Test coverage >80% for context paths
title: Add cancellation and timeout tests

description:
## Problem

Beads history grows without bound. Every message, handoff, work assignment
stays in issues.jsonl forever. Enterprise users will balk at "git as database."

## Solution: Two-Tier Cleanup

### Tier 1: Ephemeral Cleanup (v1)

bd cleanup --ephemeral --closed

- Deletes closed issues where ephemeral=true from issues.jsonl
- Safe: only removes explicitly marked ephemeral + closed
- Preserves git history (commits still exist)
- Run after swarm completion

### Tier 2: History Compaction (v2)

bd compact --squash

- Rewrites issues.jsonl to remove tombstones
- Optionally squashes git history (interactive rebase equivalent)
- Preserves Merkle proofs for deleted items
- Advanced: cold storage tiering

## HOP Context

| Layer | HOP Role | Persistence |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| Execution trace | None | Ephemeral |
| Work scaffolding | None | Summarizable |
| Work outcome | CV entry | Permanent |
| Validation record | Stake proof | Permanent |

"Execution is ephemeral. Outcomes are permanent. You can't squash your CV."

## Success Criteria

- After cleanup --ephemeral: issues.jsonl only contains persistent work
- Work outcomes preserved (CV entries)
- Validation records preserved (stake proofs)
- Execution scaffolding removed (transient coordination)

```go
// template.go:474
newIssue := &types.Issue{
    Title:      substituteVariables(oldIssue.Title, vars),
    // ... existing fields ...
    Ephemeral:  true,  // ADD THIS LINE
}
```

## Optional: Add --persistent flag

Add flag to bd mol spawn for when you want spawned issues to persist:

```bash
bd mol spawn mol-code-review --var pr=123  # ephemeral (default)
bd mol spawn mol-code-review --var pr=123 --persistent  # not ephemeral
```

## Why This Is Simpler Than Original Design

description:
Add bd mol squash command for basic molecule execution compression.

## Command

bd mol squash <molecule-id> [flags]
  --dry-run         Preview what would be squashed
  --keep-children   Don't delete ephemeral children after squash
  --json            JSON output

## Implementation

1. Find all ephemeral children of molecule (parent-child deps)
2. Concatenate child descriptions/notes into digest
3. Create digest issue in main repo with:
   - Title: 'Molecule Execution Summary: <original-title>'
   - digest_of: [list of squashed child IDs]
   - ephemeral: false (digest is permanent)
4. Delete ephemeral children (unless --keep-children)
5. Link digest to parent work item

## Schema Changes

Add to Issue struct:
- SquashedAt *time.Time
- SquashDigest string (ID of digest)
- DigestOf []string (IDs of squashed children)

## Acceptance Criteria

- bd mol squash <id> creates digest, removes children
- --dry-run shows preview
- Digest has proper metadata linking
title: Tier 2: Basic bd mol squash command

description:
## Design: Agent-Provided Summarization (Inversion of Control)

bd is a tool FOR agents, not an agent itself. The calling agent provides
the summary; bd just stores it.

### API

```bash
# Agent generates summary, passes to bd
bd mol squash bd-xxx --summary "Agent-generated summary here"

# Without --summary, falls back to basic concatenation
bd mol squash bd-xxx
```

### Gas Town Integration Pattern

```go
// In polecat completion handler or witness
raw := exec.Command("bd", "mol", "show", molID, "--json").Output()
summary := callHaiku(buildSummaryPrompt(raw))  // agent's job
exec.Command("bd", "mol", "squash", molID, "--summary", summary).Run()
```

### Why This Design

| Concern | bd's job | Agent's job |
|---------|----------|-------------|
| Store data | ✅ | |
| Query data | ✅ | |
| Generate summaries | | ✅ |
| Call LLMs | | ✅ |
| Manage API keys | | ✅ |

### Implementation Status

- [x] --summary flag added to bd mol squash
- [x] Tests for agent-provided summary
- [ ] Gas Town integration (separate task)

### Acceptance Criteria

- ✅ bd mol squash --summary uses provided text
- ✅ Without --summary, falls back to concatenation
- ✅ No LLM calls in bd itself
title: Tier 3: AI-powered squash summarization

description:
Automatically squash molecules when they reach terminal state.

## Integration Points

1. Hook into molecule completion handler
2. Detect when all steps are done/failed
3. Trigger squash automatically

## Config

bd config set mol.auto_squash true  # Default: false
bd config set mol.auto_squash_on_success true  # Only on success
bd config set mol.auto_squash_delay '5m'  # Wait before squash

## Implementation Options

### Option A: Post-Completion Hook
In mol completion handler:
- Check if auto_squash enabled
- Call Squash() after terminal state

### Option B: Git Hook
In .beads/hooks/post-commit:
- bd mol squash --auto

### Option C: Daemon Background Task
- Daemon periodically checks for squashable molecules
- Squashes in background

## Acceptance Criteria

- Completed molecules auto-squash without manual intervention
- Configurable delay before squash
- Option to squash only on success vs always
- Works with both daemon and no-daemon modes
title: Tier 4: Auto-squash on molecule completion

description:
Periodic rotation of issues.jsonl for long-running repos.

## Design

.beads/
├── issues.jsonl           # Current (hot)
├── archive/
│   ├── issues-2025-12.jsonl.gz  # Archived (cold)
│   └── ...
└── index.jsonl            # Merged index for queries

## Commands

bd archive rotate [flags]
  --older-than N    Archive issues closed > N days
  --compress        Gzip archives
  --dry-run         Preview

bd archive list           # Show archived periods
bd archive restore <period>  # Restore from archive

## Config

bd config set archive.enabled true
bd config set archive.rotate_days 90
bd config set archive.compress true
bd config set archive.path '.beads/archive'

## Considerations

- Archives can be gitignored (local only) or committed (shared)
- Query layer must check index, hydrate from archive
- Cold storage tiering (S3/GCS) for enterprise
- Merkle proofs preserved for audit

## Priority

This is post-1.0 work. Current focus is on squash (removes ephemeral).
Archive helps with long-term history but is less critical.
title: Tier 5 (Future): JSONL archive rotation

description:

**Symptom:** CloseIssue fails with "FOREIGN KEY constraint failed (787)" when called via MCP/daemon, but works fine via CLI.

**Root Cause:** Unknown - likely stricter FK enforcement in SQLite 3.50.4 or modernc.org wrapper changes.

**Workaround:** Pinned to v1.38.2 (SQLite 3.49.x)

**TODO:**
1. Monitor modernc.org/sqlite releases for fixes
2. Check SQLite 3.50.5+ changelogs for FK-related fixes
3. Investigate why daemon mode fails but CLI succeeds (connection reuse? transaction isolation?)
4. Consider filing upstream issue with reproducible test case
5. Upgrade when safe
title: Investigate and upgrade to modernc.org/sqlite 1.39.1+

description:
The activity command (activity.go) references rich mutation event types (MutationBonded, MutationSquashed, MutationBurned, MutationStatus) that include metadata like OldStatus, NewStatus, ParentID, and StepCount.

However, the emitMutation() function in server_core.go:141 only accepts (eventType, issueID) and only populates Type, IssueID, and Timestamp. The additional metadata fields are never set.

Need to either:
1. Add an emitRichMutation() function that accepts the additional metadata

description:
Epic to fix the N-way collision convergence problem documented in n-way-collision-convergence.md.

## Problem Summary
The current collision resolution implementation works correctly for 2-way collisions but does not converge for 3-way (and by extension N-way) collisions. TestThreeCloneCollision demonstrates this with reproducible failures.

## Root Causes Identified
1. Pairwise resolution doesn't scale - each clone makes local decisions without global context
2. DetectCollisions modifies state during detection (line 83-86 in collision.go)
3. No remapping history - can't track transitive remap chains (test-1 → test-2 → test-3)
4. Import-time resolution is too late - happens after git merge

## Solution Architecture
Replace pairwise resolution with deterministic global N-way resolution using:
- Content-addressable identity (content hashing)
- Global collision resolution (sort all versions by hash)
- Read-only detection phase (separate from modification)
- Idempotent imports (content-first matching)

## Success Criteria
- TestThreeCloneCollision passes without skipping
- All clones converge to identical content after final pull
- No data loss (all issues present in all clones)
- Works for N workers (test with 5+ clones)
- Idempotent imports (importing same JSONL multiple times is safe)

## Implementation Phases
See child issues for detailed breakdown of each phase.
title: Fix N-way collision convergence

description:
Agent in ~/src/wyvern discovered 4 simultaneous daemon processes running, causing infinite directory recursion (.beads/.beads/.beads/...). Each daemon used relative paths and created nested .beads/ directories.

Root cause: No singleton enforcement. Multiple `bd daemon` processes can start in same workspace.

Expected: One daemon per workspace (each workspace = separate .beads/ dir with bd.sock)
Actual: Multiple daemons can run simultaneously in same workspace

Note: Separate git clones = separate workspaces = separate daemons (correct). Git worktrees share .beads/ and have known limitations (documented, use --no-daemon).
notes:
## Fix Summary

Successfully prevented the nested .beads/.beads/ recursion bug by implementing two safeguards:

1. **Path Canonicalization in FindDatabasePath()** (beads.go):
   - Added filepath.Abs() + filepath.EvalSymlinks() to normalize all database paths
   - Prevents relative path edge cases that create nested directories
   - Ensures all daemons see the same canonical path

2. **Nested Directory Detection** (daemon_lifecycle.go):
   - Added explicit check for ".beads/.beads" pattern in setupDaemonLock()
   - Fails fast with clear error message if nested structure detected
   - Provides user hints about proper usage

## Root Cause

The daemon lock (added Oct 22, 2025) correctly prevents simultaneous daemons in the SAME workspace. However, when BEADS_DB used a relative path (e.g., ".beads/beads.db") from inside the .beads directory, FindDatabasePath() would resolve it to a nested path creating a separate workspace:
- First daemon: /workspace/.beads/beads.db
- Second daemon from .beads/: /workspace/.beads/.beads/beads.db ← Different lock file!

## Testing

All acceptance criteria passed:
✅ 1. Second daemon start fails with "daemon already running" error
✅ 2. Killing daemon releases lock, new daemon can start  
✅ 3. No infinite .beads/ recursion possible (tested nested BEADS_DB path)
✅ 4. Works with auto-start mechanism

The fix addresses the edge case while maintaining the existing lock mechanism's correctness.
title: Enforce daemon singleton per workspace with file locking

description:
When daemon detects multiple .db files (after filtering out .backup and vc.db files), it writes error details to .beads/daemon-error file before exiting.

The error file is checked when:
1. Daemon discovery fails to connect (internal/daemon/discovery.go)
2. Auto-start fails to yield a running daemon (cmd/bd/main.go)
3. Daemon list shows 'daemon not responding' error

This makes the error immediately visible to users without requiring them to check daemon logs.

File created:  cmd/bd/daemon.go (writes daemon-error on multiple .db detection)
Files modified: 
- internal/daemon/discovery.go (reads daemon-error and surfaces in DaemonInfo.Error)
- cmd/bd/main.go (displays daemon-error when auto-start fails)

Testing: Create multiple .db files in .beads/, start daemon, verify error file created and shown in bd daemons list
notes:
Root cause: Daemon exits with os.Exit(1) when multiple .db files detected (daemon.go:1381), but error only goes to daemon log file. User sees 'daemon not responding' without knowing why.

Current detection:
- daemon.go filters out .backup and vc.db files
- bd doctor detects multiple databases
- Error message tells user to run 'bd init' or manually remove

Problem: Error is not user-visible unless they check daemon logs.

Proposed fix options:
1. Surface the error in 'bd info' and 'bd daemons list' output
2. Add a hint in error messages to run 'bd doctor' when daemon fails
3. Make daemon write error to a .beads/daemon-error file that gets checked
4. Improve 'bd doctor' to run automatically when daemon is unhealthy
title: Daemon crashes silently when multiple .db files exist in .beads/

description:
hasJSONLChanged() reads entire JSONL file to compute SHA256 hash on every check. For large databases (50MB+), this is expensive and called frequently by daemon.

Optimization: Check mtime first as fast-path. Only compute hash if mtime changed. This catches git operations (mtime changes but content doesn't) while avoiding expensive hash computation in common case (mtime unchanged = content unchanged).

Performance impact: 99% of checks will use fast path, only git operations need slow path.
title: Add performance optimization to hasJSONLChanged with mtime fast-path

description:

2. Roll back SQLite metadata to pre-export state
3. Optionally restore JSONL from git HEAD
4. Report clear error with recovery instructions

This makes sync failures recoverable without manual intervention.

Depends on the core atomicity fix - this is defense in depth.
title: Add sync rollback on git commit failure

description:

Add support for reading message body from stdin:
- gt mail send addr -s 'Subject' --stdin  # Read body from stdin
- echo 'body' | gt mail send addr -s 'Subject' -m -  # Convention: -m - means stdin

This would allow:
cat <<'EOF' | gt mail send addr -s 'Subject' --stdin
Message with `backticks` and 'quotes' safely
EOF

Without this, agents struggle to send handoff messages containing code snippets.
title: gt mail send: support reading message body from stdin

description:
The current collision resolution uses creation timestamps to decide which issue to keep vs. remap. This is non-deterministic when two clones create issues at nearly the same time.

Root cause of bd-71107098:
- Clone A creates test-1="Issue from clone A" at T0
- Clone B creates test-1="Issue from clone B" at T0+30ms
- Clone B syncs first, remaps Clone A's to test-2
- Clone A syncs second, sees collision, remaps Clone B's to test-2
- Result: titles are swapped between clones

Solution:
- Use content-based hashing (title + description + priority + type)
- Deterministic winner: always keep issue with lower hash
- Same collision on different clones produces same result (idempotent)

Implementation:
- Modify ScoreCollisions in internal/storage/sqlite/collision.go
- Replace timestamp-based scoring with content hash comparison
- Ensure hash function is stable across platforms
title: Implement content-hash based collision resolution for deterministic convergence
File change test issue

description:
## Problem

The sync sanitize process incorrectly identifies newly created issues as 'deleted issues resurrected by git merge' and removes them from the local JSONL file.

## Reproduction

1. Create a new issue: bd create --title='Test issue'
2. Run bd sync
3. Observe: New issue appears in sanitize removal list
4. Issue is removed from local JSONL but preserved on beads-sync branch

## Observed Behavior

During sync, the sanitize step outputs:
```
→ Sanitized JSONL: removed 738 deleted issue(s) that were resurrected by git merge
  - bd-08ea  (newly created issue!)
  - bd-tnsq  (newly created issue!)
  ...
```

The newly created issues get removed locally but remain on beads-sync branch.

## Expected Behavior

Newly created issues should NOT be removed by sanitize. The sanitize should only remove issues that:
1. Were previously deleted (have tombstones or are in deletions manifest)
2. Are being resurrected from old git history

## Root Cause Investigation

The sanitize logic likely compares the local DB snapshot against some reference and incorrectly classifies new issues as 'resurrected deleted issues'. Possible causes:
- Snapshot protection logic not accounting for new issues
- Deletion manifest containing stale entries
- Race condition between export and sanitize

## Impact

- New issues disappear from local JSONL after sync
- Issues remain on beads-sync but cause confusion
- Multi-agent workflows affected when agents can't see new issues locally
- Requires manual intervention to recover

## Files to Investigate

- cmd/bd/sync.go - sanitize logic
- cmd/bd/snapshot_manager.go - snapshot comparison
- Deletion manifest handling

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Newly created issues survive sync without being sanitized
- [ ] Only truly deleted/resurrected issues are removed
- [ ] Add test case for this scenario
title: Sync sanitize incorrectly removes newly created issues

- `close.go` only uses `utils.ResolvePartialIDs()` without routing

- reopen
- dep  
- comments
- label
- duplicate
- defer/undefer

**Fix:**
Add routing support to `close.go` similar to `show.go` lines 44-147.

```go
var adminCmd = &cobra.Command{
    Use:   "admin",
    Short: "Administrative commands for database maintenance",
    Long: `Administrative commands for beads database maintenance.

These commands are for advanced users and should be used carefully:
  cleanup   Delete closed issues and prune expired tombstones
  compact   Compact old closed issues to save space
  reset     Remove all beads data and configuration

For routine operations, prefer 'bd doctor --fix'.`,
}

func init() {
    rootCmd.AddCommand(adminCmd)
    adminCmd.AddCommand(cleanupCmd)
    adminCmd.AddCommand(compactCmd)

}
```

description:
Implement bd mol advance for stepping through molecules.

## Command

```bash
bd mol advance <mol-id>
```

## Behavior

1. Load molecule state from .beads/molecules/<mol-id>.state.yaml
2. Find current step
3. Mark current step completed
4. Find next available step (respecting needs/deps)
5. Update state file
6. Output next step info (or COMPLETE if done)

## Step State Machine

```
pending → in_progress → completed
```

When advancing:
- Current step: in_progress → completed
- Next step: pending → in_progress

## Output

```bash
$ bd mol advance mol-deacon-patrol
✓ Step inbox-check completed
→ Next step: spawn-work

Title: Spawn polecat for ready work
Description: ...
```

Or if complete:
```bash
$ bd mol advance mol-deacon-patrol
✓ Step self-inspect completed
✓ Molecule COMPLETE

Run bd mol reset to loop, or gt handoff to cycle.
```

## Files

- cmd/bd/mol_advance.go
- internal/mol/state.go
title: bd mol advance: Step through molecule execution

description:
## Problem

`bd init` scans git history to find all issues ever created, which:
- Resurrects deleted/compacted issues
- Takes a long time on large repos
- Defeats manual JSONL cleanup

## Solution

Add `bd init --from-jsonl` flag that:
1. Creates fresh database
2. Imports ONLY from current .beads/issues.jsonl
3. Skips git history scan entirely

## Use Case

After manually cleaning JSONL or receiving a clean JSONL from another source:
```bash
# Clean JSONL manually or via script
bd init --from-jsonl  # Use current JSONL as-is
```

(Moved from hq-c21fj)
title: bd init --from-jsonl: Skip git history scan

description:
Enable beads to work in Claude Code Web sandboxes by compiling bd to WebAssembly.

## Problem
Claude Code Web sandboxes cannot install bd CLI due to network restrictions:
- GitHub releases return 403
- go install fails with DNS errors
- Binary cannot be downloaded

## Solution
Compile bd Go codebase to WASM, publish to npm as drop-in replacement.

## Technical Approach
- Use GOOS=js GOARCH=wasm to compile bd

description:
When `bd cook` creates a proto, it uses the formula name as the proto ID:

```yaml
formula: mol-deacon-patrol
```

Creates proto with ID `mol-deacon-patrol`.

## Problem

If the project uses a different prefix (e.g., `gt-`), the cooked protos have 
a different prefix (`mol-`), causing:

1. `bd sync` import warnings about prefix mismatch
2. Mixed prefix lists in `bd mol list`
3. Confusion about naming conventions

## Options

1. **Use project prefix**: Cook as `gt-mol-deacon-patrol`
2. **Allow mol- as special prefix**: Recognize mol-* as valid for protos
3. **Make prefix configurable**: `bd cook --prefix gt-`
4. **Keep current behavior**: Document that formulas use their own namespace

## Current Workaround

The prefix mismatch warning can be ignored, but `bd sync` fails the import step.

## Related

gt-i6k1: Duplicate proto cleanup
title: bd cook: Proto ID prefix should match project prefix

description:
The current collision resolution uses creation timestamps to decide which issue to keep vs. remap. This is non-deterministic when two clones create issues at nearly the same time.

Root cause of bd-71107098:
- Clone A creates test-1="Issue from clone A" at T0
- Clone B creates test-1="Issue from clone B" at T0+30ms
- Clone B syncs first, remaps Clone A's to test-2
- Clone A syncs second, sees collision, remaps Clone B's to test-2
- Result: titles are swapped between clones

Solution:
- Use content-based hashing (title + description + priority + type)
- Deterministic winner: always keep issue with lower hash
- Same collision on different clones produces same result (idempotent)

Implementation:
- Modify ScoreCollisions in internal/storage/sqlite/collision.go
- Replace timestamp-based scoring with content hash comparison
- Ensure hash function is stable across platforms
notes:
Rename detection successfully implemented and tested!

**What was implemented:**
1. Content-hash based rename detection in DetectCollisions
2. When importing JSONL, if an issue has different ID but same content as DB issue, treat as rename
3. Delete old ID and accept new ID from JSONL
4. Added post-import re-export in sync command to flush rename changes
5. Added post-import commit to capture rename changes

**Test results:**
TestTwoCloneCollision now shows full convergence:
- Clone A: test-2="Issue from clone A", test-1="Issue from clone B"
- Clone B: test-1="Issue from clone B", test-2="Issue from clone A"

Both clones have **identical content** (titles match IDs correctly). Only timestamps differ (expected).

**What remains:**
- Test still expects exact JSON match including timestamps
- Could normalize timestamp comparison, but content convergence is the critical success metric
- The two-clone collision workflow now works without data corruption!
title: Implement content-hash based collision resolution for deterministic convergence

description:
When 'bd prime' runs on a branch with no upstream (ephemeral branch), it should output a different SESSION CLOSE PROTOCOL.

**Current output (wrong for ephemeral branches):**
```
[ ] 1. git status
[ ] 2. git add <files>
[ ] 3. bd sync
[ ] 4. git commit -m "..."
[ ] 5. bd sync
[ ] 6. git push
```

**Needed output for ephemeral branches:**
```
[ ] 1. git status
[ ] 2. git add <files>
[ ] 3. bd sync --from-main  (pull updates from main)
[ ] 4. git commit -m "..."
[ ] 5. (no push - branch is ephemeral)
```

**Detection:** `git rev-parse --abbrev-ref --symbolic-full-name @{u}` returns error code 128 if no upstream.

Also update Sync & Collaboration section to mention `bd sync --from-main` for ephemeral branches.

**Use case:** Gastown polecats work on ephemeral local branches that are never pushed. Their code gets merged to main via local merge, and beads changes stay local (communicated via gm mail to Overseer).
title: bd prime: Detect ephemeral branches and adjust workflow output

description:
## Problem

To create an issue in a different rig, you must cd to that rig's directory. This is clunky when coordinating across rigs.

## Proposed Solution

Add a --rig flag to bd create that leverages existing routes.jsonl:

```bash
# From anywhere in town
bd create --rig beads --title="bug report" --type=bug

# Equivalent to:
cd ~/gt/beads/mayor/rig && bd create --title="..."
```

## How it works

1. Parse --rig flag (rig name, not prefix)
2. Look up rig in routes.jsonl to find beads location
3. Determine prefix from rig config
4. Create issue in that location with auto-generated ID

## Why elegant

- Leverages existing routing infrastructure
- Rig names are human-memorable (not cryptic prefixes)
- Works from anywhere in town
- Prefix auto-determined from rig config
- Reads naturally: "create in beads"

## Alternative

Also consider --prefix for power users who know prefixes:
```bash
bd create --prefix bd "Title here"
```
title: bd create --rig flag for cross-rig issue filing
