description:
Replace sequential auto-increment IDs (bd-1c63eb84, bd-9063acda) with content-hash based IDs (bd-af78e9a2) plus human-friendly aliases (#1, #2).

## Motivation
Current sequential IDs cause collision problems when multiple clones work offline:
- Non-deterministic convergence in N-way scenarios (bd-cbed9619.1, bd-e6d71828)
- Complex collision resolution logic (~2,100 LOC)
- UNIQUE constraint violations during import
- Requires coordination between workers

Hash-based IDs eliminate collisions entirely while aliases preserve human readability.

## Benefits
- ✅ Collision-free distributed ID generation
- ✅ Eliminates ~2,100 LOC of collision handling code
- ✅ Better git merge behavior (different IDs = different JSONL lines)
- ✅ True offline-first workflows
- ✅ Simpler auto-import (no remapping needed)
- ✅ Enables parallel CI/CD workers without coordination

## Design
- Canonical ID: bd-af78e9a2 (8-char SHA256 prefix of title+desc+timestamp+creator)
- Alias: #42 (auto-increment per workspace, mutable, display-only)
- CLI accepts both: bd show bd-af78e9a2 OR bd show #42
- JSONL stores hash IDs only (aliases reconstructed on import)
- Alias conflicts resolved via content-hash ordering (deterministic)

## Breaking Change
This is a v2.0 feature requiring migration. Provide bd migrate --hash-ids tool.

## Timeline
~9 weeks (Phase 1: Hash IDs 4w, Phase 2: Aliases 3w, Phase 3: Testing 2w)

## Dependencies
Should complete after bd-7c5915ae (cleanup validation) and before bd-710a4916 (CRDT).
title: Hash-based IDs with aliasing system

description:
Final validation: migrate the beads project itself to hash-based IDs.

## Purpose
Dogfooding the migration on beads' own issue database to:
1. Validate migration tool works on real data
2. Discover edge cases
3. Verify all workflows still work
4. Build confidence for users

## Pre-Migration Checklist
- [ ] All bd-f8b764c9 child tasks completed
- [ ] All tests pass: `go test ./...`
- [ ] Migration tool tested on test databases
- [ ] Documentation updated
- [ ] MCP server updated and published
- [ ] Clean git status

## Migration Steps

### 1. Create Backup
```bash
# Backup database
cp -r .beads .beads.backup-1761798568

# Backup JSONL
cp .beads/beads.jsonl .beads/beads.jsonl.backup

# Create git branch for migration
git checkout -b hash-id-migration
git add .beads.backup-*
git commit -m "Pre-migration backup"
```

### 2. Run Migration (Dry Run)
```bash
bd migrate --hash-ids --dry-run > migration-plan.txt
cat migration-plan.txt

# Review:
# - Number of issues to migrate
# - Hash collision check (should be zero)
# - Text reference updates
# - Dependency updates
```

### 3. Run Migration (Real)
```bash
bd migrate --hash-ids 2>&1 | tee migration-log.txt

# Expected output:
# ✓ Backup created: .beads/beads.db.backup-1234567890
# ✓ Generated 150 hash IDs
# ✓ No hash collisions detected
# ✓ Updated issues table schema
# ✓ Updated 150 issue IDs
# ✓ Updated 87 dependencies
# ✓ Updated 234 text references
# ✓ Exported to .beads/beads.jsonl
# ✓ Migration complete!
```

### 4. Validation

#### Database Integrity
```bash
# Check all issues have hash IDs
bd list | grep -v "bd-[a-f0-9]\{8\}" && echo "FAIL: Non-hash IDs found"

# Check all issues have aliases
sqlite3 .beads/beads.db "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM issues WHERE alias IS NULL"
# Should be 0

# Check no alias duplicates
sqlite3 .beads/beads.db "SELECT alias, COUNT(*) FROM issues GROUP BY alias HAVING COUNT(*) > 1"
# Should be empty
```

#### Functionality Tests
```bash
# Test show by hash ID
bd show bd-

# Test show by alias
bd show #1

# Test create new issue
bd create "Test issue after migration" -p 2
# Should get hash ID + alias

# Test update
bd update #1 --priority 1

# Test dependencies
bd dep tree #1

# Test export
bd export
git diff .beads/beads.jsonl
# Should show hash IDs
```

#### Text Reference Validation
```bash
# Check that old IDs were updated in descriptions
grep -r "bd-[0-9]\{1,3\}[^a-f0-9]" .beads/beads.jsonl && echo "FAIL: Old ID format found"

# Verify hash ID references exist
grep -o "bd-[a-f0-9]\{8\}" .beads/beads.jsonl | sort -u | wc -l
# Should match number of hash IDs
```

### 5. Commit Migration
```bash
git add .beads/beads.jsonl .beads/beads.db
git commit -m "Migrate to hash-based IDs (v2.0)

- Migrated 150 issues to hash IDs
- Preserved aliases (#1-#150)
- Updated 87 dependencies
- Updated 234 text references
- Zero hash collisions

Migration log: migration-log.txt"

git push origin hash-id-migration
```

### 6. Create PR
```bash
gh pr create --title "Migrate to hash-based IDs (v2.0)" --body "## Summary
Migrates beads project to hash-based IDs as part of v2.0 release.

## Migration Stats
- Issues migrated: 150
- Dependencies updated: 87
- Text references updated: 234
- Hash collisions: 0
- Aliases assigned: 150

## Validation
- ✅ All tests pass
- ✅ Database integrity verified
- ✅ All workflows tested (show, update, create, deps)
- ✅ Text references updated correctly
- ✅ Export produces valid JSONL

## Files Changed
- `.beads/beads.jsonl` - Hash IDs in all entries
- `.beads/beads.db` - Schema updated with aliases

## Rollback
If issues arise:
\`\`\`bash
mv .beads.backup-1234567890 .beads
bd export
\`\`\`

See migration-log.txt for full details."
```

### 7. Merge and Cleanup
```bash
# After PR approval
git checkout main
git merge hash-id-migration
git push origin main

# Tag release
git tag v2.0.0
git push origin v2.0.0

# Cleanup
rm migration-log.txt migration-plan.txt
git checkout .beads.backup-*  # Keep in git history
```

## Rollback Procedure
If migration fails or has issues:

```bash
# Restore backup
mv .beads .beads.failed-migration
mv .beads.backup-1234567890 .beads

# Regenerate JSONL
bd export

# Verify restoration
bd list
git diff .beads/beads.jsonl

# Cleanup
git checkout hash-id-migration
git reset --hard main
```

## Post-Migration Communication

### GitHub Issue/Discussion
```markdown
## Beads v2.0 Released: Hash-Based IDs

We've migrated beads to hash-based IDs! 🎉

**What changed:**
- Issues now use hash IDs (bd-af78e9a2) instead of sequential (bd-cb64c226.3)
- Human-friendly aliases (#42) for easy reference
- Zero collision risk in distributed workflows

**Action required:**
If you have a local clone, you need to migrate:

\`\`\`bash
git pull origin main
bd migrate --hash-ids
git push origin main
\`\`\`

See MIGRATION.md for details.

**Benefits:**
- ✅ No more ID collisions
- ✅ Work offline without coordination
- ✅ Simpler codebase (-2,100 LOC)

Questions? Reply here or see docs/HASH_IDS.md
```

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Migration completes without errors
- [ ] All validation checks pass
- [ ] PR merged to main
- [ ] v2.0.0 tagged and released
- [ ] Documentation updated
- [ ] Community notified
- [ ] No rollback needed within 1 week

## Files to Create
- migration-log.txt (transient)
- migration-plan.txt (transient)

## Timeline
Execute after all other bd-f8b764c9 tasks complete (estimated: ~8 weeks from start)

## API Changes

```

title: Add alias field to database schema

description:
Design and specify the hash-based ID generation algorithm.

## Requirements
- Deterministic: same inputs → same ID
- Collision-resistant: ~2^32 space for 8-char hex
- Fast: <1μs per generation
- Includes timestamp for uniqueness
- Includes creator/workspace for distributed uniqueness

## Proposed Algorithm
```go
func GenerateIssueID(title, desc string, created time.Time, workspaceID string) string {
    h := sha256.New()
    h.Write([]byte(title))
    h.Write([]byte(desc))
    h.Write([]byte(created.Format(time.RFC3339Nano)))
    h.Write([]byte(workspaceID))
    hash := hex.EncodeToString(h.Sum(nil))
    return "bd-" + hash[:8]  // 8-char prefix = 2^32 space
}
```

## Open Questions
1. 8 chars (2^32) or 16 chars (2^64) for collision resistance?
2. Include priority/type in hash? (Pro: more entropy. Con: immutable)
3. How to handle workspace ID generation? (hostname? UUID?)
4. What if title+desc change? (Answer: ID stays same - hash only used at creation)

## Deliverables
- Design doc: docs/HASH_ID_DESIGN.md
- Collision probability analysis
- Performance benchmarks
- Prototype implementation in internal/types/id_generator.go
title: Design hash ID generation algorithm

description:
Update all documentation to explain hash-based IDs and aliasing system.

## Files to Update

### 1. README.md
Add section explaining hash IDs:
```markdown
## Issue IDs

Beads uses **hash-based IDs** for collision-free distributed issue tracking:

- **Hash ID**: `bd-af78e9a2` (8-char SHA256 prefix, immutable, globally unique)
- **Alias**: `#42` (sequential number, mutable, human-friendly)

### Using IDs
```bash
bd show bd-af78e9a2   # Use hash ID
bd show #42           # Use alias
bd show 42            # Use alias (shorthand)
```

### Why Hash IDs?
- **Collision-free**: Work offline without ID conflicts
- **Distributed**: No coordination needed between clones
- **Git-friendly**: Different IDs = different JSONL lines, fewer merge conflicts

### Aliases
Aliases are workspace-local shortcuts for hash IDs. They're:
- Automatically assigned on issue creation
- Reassigned deterministically on sync (if conflicts)
- Can be manually controlled with `bd alias` commands
```

### 2. AGENTS.md
Update agent workflow:
```markdown
## Hash-Based IDs (v2.0+)

Beads v2.0 uses hash-based IDs to eliminate collision problems:

**When creating issues**:
```bash
bd create "Fix bug" -p 1
# → Creates bd-af78e9a2 with alias #1
```

**When referencing issues**:
- In text: Use hash IDs (stable): "See bd-af78e9a2 for details"
- In CLI: Use aliases (readable): `bd update #42 --status done`

**After sync**:
- Alias conflicts resolved automatically (content-hash ordering)
- No ID collisions possible
- No remapping needed

**Migration from v1.x**:
```bash
bd migrate --hash-ids  # One-time migration
```
```

### 3. QUICKSTART.md (if exists)
Show alias usage in examples:
```bash
# Create issue (gets hash ID + alias)
bd create "Fix authentication bug" -p 1
# → Created bd-af78e9a2 (alias: #1)

# Reference by alias
bd show #1
bd update #1 --status in_progress
bd close #1 --reason "Fixed"
```

### 4. ADVANCED.md
Add section on hash ID internals:
```markdown
## Hash ID Generation

Hash IDs are generated deterministically:

```go
SHA256(title || description || timestamp || workspace_id)[:8]
```

**Collision probability**:
- 8 hex chars = 2^32 space = ~4 billion IDs
- Birthday paradox: 50% collision probability at ~65,000 issues
- For typical projects (<10,000 issues), collision risk is negligible

**Collision detection**:
If a hash collision occurs (extremely rare), beads:
1. Detects on insert (UNIQUE constraint)
2. Appends random suffix: `bd-af78e9a2-a1b2`
3. Retries insert

## Alias Conflict Resolution

When multiple clones assign same alias to different issues:

**Strategy**: Content-hash ordering (deterministic)
- Sort conflicting issue IDs lexicographically
- Lowest hash ID keeps the alias
- Others reassigned to next available aliases

**Example**:
```
Clone A: Assigns #42 to bd-a1b2c3d4
Clone B: Assigns #42 to bd-e5f6a7b8
After sync: bd-a1b2c3d4 keeps #42 (lower hash)
            bd-e5f6a7b8 gets #100 (next available)
```
```

### 5. MIGRATION.md (new file)
```markdown
# Migrating to Hash-Based IDs (v2.0)

## Overview
Beads v2.0 introduces hash-based IDs to eliminate collision problems. This is a **breaking change** requiring migration.

## Migration Steps

### 1. Backup
```bash
cp -r .beads .beads.backup
git commit -am "Pre-migration backup"
```

### 2. Run Migration
```bash
# Dry run first
bd migrate --hash-ids --dry-run

# Apply migration
bd migrate --hash-ids
```

### 3. Commit Changes
```bash
git add .beads/issues.jsonl
git commit -m "Migrate to hash-based IDs (v2.0)"
git push origin main
```

### 4. Coordinate with Collaborators
All clones must migrate before syncing:
1. Notify team: "Migrating to v2.0 on [date]"
2. All collaborators pull latest
3. All run `bd migrate --hash-ids`
4. All push changes
5. Resume normal work

## Rollback
```bash
# Restore backup
mv .beads.backup .beads
bd export  # Regenerate JSONL
git checkout .beads/issues.jsonl
```

## FAQ

**Q: Can I mix v1.x and v2.0 clones?**
A: No. All clones must be on same version.

**Q: Will my old issue IDs work?**
A: No, but aliases preserve the numbers: bd-1c63eb84 → #1

**Q: What happens to links like "see bd-cb64c226.3"?**
A: Migration updates all text references automatically.
```

### 6. CHANGELOG.md
```markdown
## v2.0.0 (YYYY-MM-DD)

### Breaking Changes
- **Hash-based IDs**: Issues now use collision-free hash IDs (bd-af78e9a2)
  instead of sequential IDs (bd-1c63eb84, bd-9063acda)
- **Aliasing system**: Human-friendly aliases (#42) for hash IDs
- **Migration required**: Run `bd migrate --hash-ids` to convert v1.x databases

### Added
- `bd alias` command for manual alias control
- `bd migrate --hash-ids` migration tool
- Alias conflict resolution (deterministic, content-hash ordering)

### Removed
- ID collision detection and resolution (~2,100 LOC)
- `bd import --resolve-collisions` flag (no longer needed)

### Benefits
- ✅ Zero ID collisions in distributed workflows
- ✅ Simpler codebase (-1,350 net LOC)
- ✅ Better git merge behavior
- ✅ True offline-first operation
```

## Testing
- Build docs locally (if using doc generator)
- Check all links work
- Verify examples are correct
- Spellcheck

## Files to Create/Modify
- README.md (hash ID section)
- AGENTS.md (workflow updates)
- ADVANCED.md (internals)
- MIGRATION.md (new)
- CHANGELOG.md (v2.0 entry)
- docs/ (any other docs)
title: Update documentation for hash IDs and aliases

description:
Add command for users to manually view and reassign aliases.

## Command: bd alias

### Subcommands

#### 1. bd alias list
Show all alias mappings:
```bash
$ bd alias list
#1    → bd-af78e9a2  Fix authentication bug
#2    → bd-e5f6a7b8  Add logging to daemon
#42   → bd-1a2b3c4d  Investigate jujutsu integration
#100  → bd-9a8b7c6d  (reassigned after conflict)
```

#### 2. bd alias set <alias> <hash-id>
Manually assign alias to specific issue:
```bash
$ bd alias set 42 bd-1a2b3c4d
✓ Assigned alias #42 to bd-1a2b3c4d

$ bd alias set 1 bd-af78e9a2
✗ Error: Alias #1 already assigned to bd-e5f6a7b8
Use --force to override
```

#### 3. bd alias compact
Renumber all aliases to fill gaps:
```bash
$ bd alias compact [--dry-run]

Current aliases: #1, #2, #5, #7, #100, #101
After compacting: #1, #2, #3, #4, #5, #6

Renumbering:
  #5   → #3
  #7   → #4
  #100 → #5
  #101 → #6

Apply changes? [y/N]
```

#### 4. bd alias reset
Regenerate all aliases (sequential from 1):
```bash
$ bd alias reset [--sort-by=priority|created|id]

WARNING: This will reassign ALL aliases. Continue? [y/N]

Reassigning 150 issues by priority:
  bd-a1b2c3d4 → #1   (P0: Critical security bug)
  bd-e5f6a7b8 → #2   (P0: Data loss fix)
  bd-1a2b3c4d → #3   (P1: Jujutsu integration)
  ...
```

#### 5. bd alias find <hash-id>
Look up alias for hash ID:
```bash
$ bd alias find bd-af78e9a2
#1

$ bd alias find bd-nonexistent
✗ Error: Issue not found
```

## Use Cases

### 1. Keep Important Issues Low-Numbered
```bash
# After closing many P0 issues, compact to free low numbers
bd alias compact

# Or manually set
bd alias set 1 bd-<critical-bug-hash>
```

### 2. Consistent Aliases Across Clones
```bash
# After migration, coordinator assigns canonical aliases
bd alias reset --sort-by=id
git add .beads/aliases.jsonl
git commit -m "Canonical alias assignments"
git push

# Other clones pull and adopt
git pull
bd import  # Alias conflicts resolved automatically
```

### 3. Debug Alias Conflicts
```bash
# See which aliases were reassigned
bd alias list | grep "#100"
```

## Flags

### Global
- `--dry-run`: Preview changes without applying
- `--force`: Override existing alias assignments

### bd alias reset
- `--sort-by=priority`: Assign by priority (P0 first)
- `--sort-by=created`: Assign by creation time (oldest first)
- `--sort-by=id`: Assign by hash ID (lexicographic)

## Implementation

File: cmd/bd/alias.go
```go
func aliasListCmd() *cobra.Command {
    return &cobra.Command{
        Use:   "list",
        Short: "List all alias mappings",
        Run: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
            aliases := storage.GetAllAliases()
            for _, a := range aliases {
                fmt.Printf("#%-4d → %s  %s\n", 
                    a.Alias, a.IssueID, a.Title)
            }
        },
    }
}

func aliasSetCmd() *cobra.Command {
    return &cobra.Command{
        Use:   "set <alias> <hash-id>",
        Short: "Manually assign alias to issue",
        Args:  cobra.ExactArgs(2),
        Run: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
            alias, _ := strconv.Atoi(args[0])
            hashID := args[1]
            
            force, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("force")
            if err := storage.SetAlias(alias, hashID, force); err != nil {
                fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "Error: %v\n", err)
                os.Exit(1)
            }
            fmt.Printf("✓ Assigned alias #%d to %s\n", alias, hashID)
        },
    }
}
```

## Files to Create
- cmd/bd/alias.go

## Testing
- Test alias list output
- Test alias set with/without force
- Test alias compact removes gaps
- Test alias reset with different sort orders
- Test alias find lookup

description:
Update beads-mcp server to support hash IDs and aliases.

## Changes Needed

### 1. MCP Function Signatures (No Change)
Functions already use issue IDs as strings, so they work with hash IDs:

```python
# These already work!
beads_show(issue_id: str)           # Accepts bd-af78e9a2 or #42
beads_update(issue_id: str, ...)    # Accepts both formats
beads_close(issue_ids: List[str])   # Accepts both formats
```

### 2. Add Alias Resolution Helper
File: integrations/beads-mcp/src/beads_mcp/server.py

```python
def resolve_issue_id(issue_id: str) -> str:
    """Resolve alias to hash ID if needed."""
    # Hash ID: pass through
    if issue_id.startswith('bd-') and len(issue_id) == 11:
        return issue_id
    
    # Alias: #42 or 42
    alias_str = issue_id.lstrip('#')
    try:
        alias = int(alias_str)
        # Call bd to resolve
        result = subprocess.run(
            ['bd', 'alias', 'find', f'bd-{alias}'],
            capture_output=True, text=True
        )
        if result.returncode == 0:
            return result.stdout.strip()
    except ValueError:
        pass
    
    # Invalid format
    raise ValueError(f"Invalid issue ID: {issue_id}")
```

### 3. Update Response Formatting
Show aliases in responses:

```python
@server.call_tool()
async def beads_show(issue_id: str) -> List[TextContent]:
    resolved_id = resolve_issue_id(issue_id)
    
    result = subprocess.run(['bd', 'show', resolved_id], ...)
    
    # Parse response and add alias info
    # Format: "bd-af78e9a2 (alias: #42)"
    ...
```

### 4. Add beads_alias_* Functions

```python
@server.call_tool()
async def beads_alias_list() -> List[TextContent]:
    """List all alias mappings."""
    result = subprocess.run(['bd', 'alias', 'list'], ...)
    return [TextContent(type="text", text=result.stdout)]

@server.call_tool()
async def beads_alias_set(alias: int, issue_id: str) -> List[TextContent]:
    """Manually assign alias to issue."""
    result = subprocess.run(['bd', 'alias', 'set', str(alias), issue_id], ...)
    return [TextContent(type="text", text=result.stdout)]

@server.call_tool()
async def beads_alias_compact() -> List[TextContent]:
    """Compact aliases to fill gaps."""
    result = subprocess.run(['bd', 'alias', 'compact'], ...)
    return [TextContent(type="text", text=result.stdout)]
```

### 5. Update Documentation
File: integrations/beads-mcp/README.md

```markdown
## Issue IDs (v2.0+)

The MCP server accepts both hash IDs and aliases:

```python
# Using hash IDs
await beads_show(issue_id="bd-af78e9a2")

# Using aliases
await beads_show(issue_id="#42")
await beads_show(issue_id="42")  # Shorthand
```

## Alias Management

New functions for alias control:

- `beads_alias_list()` - List all alias mappings
- `beads_alias_set(alias, issue_id)` - Manually assign alias
- `beads_alias_compact()` - Compact aliases to fill gaps

## Migration

After migrating to hash IDs:
1. Update beads-mcp: `pip install --upgrade beads-mcp`
2. Restart MCP server
3. All existing workflows continue to work
```

### 6. Version Compatibility
Detect and handle both v1.x and v2.0 formats:

```python
def detect_beads_version() -> str:
    """Detect if beads is using sequential or hash IDs."""
    result = subprocess.run(['bd', 'list', '-n', '1'], ...)
    first_id = parse_first_issue_id(result.stdout)
    
    if first_id.startswith('bd-') and len(first_id) > 11:
        return '2.0'  # Hash ID
    else:
        return '1.x'  # Sequential ID

# On startup
beads_version = detect_beads_version()
logger.info(f"Detected beads version: {beads_version}")
```

## Testing

### Unit Tests
File: integrations/beads-mcp/tests/test_hash_ids.py

```python
def test_resolve_hash_id():
    """Hash IDs pass through unchanged."""
    assert resolve_issue_id("bd-af78e9a2") == "bd-af78e9a2"

def test_resolve_alias():
    """Aliases resolve to hash IDs."""
    # Mock bd alias find command
    assert resolve_issue_id("#42") == "bd-af78e9a2"
    assert resolve_issue_id("42") == "bd-af78e9a2"

def test_invalid_id():
    """Invalid IDs raise ValueError."""
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        resolve_issue_id("invalid")
```

### Integration Tests
```python
async def test_show_with_hash_id(mcp_server):
    result = await mcp_server.beads_show(issue_id="bd-af78e9a2")
    assert "bd-af78e9a2" in result[0].text

async def test_show_with_alias(mcp_server):
    result = await mcp_server.beads_show(issue_id="#42")
    assert "bd-af78e9a2" in result[0].text  # Resolved
```

## Backward Compatibility
The MCP server should work with both:
- Beads v1.x (sequential IDs)
- Beads v2.0+ (hash IDs)

Detection happens at runtime based on issue ID format.

cd integrations/beads-mcp

description:
Create migration tool to convert existing sequential-ID databases to hash-ID format.

## Command: bd migrate --hash-ids

```bash
bd migrate --hash-ids [--dry-run]
```

## Migration Process

### 1. Backup Database
```bash
cp .beads/beads.db .beads/beads.db.backup-1761798384
echo "✓ Backup created: .beads/beads.db.backup-1234567890"
```

### 2. Generate Hash IDs for Existing Issues
```go
func migrateToHashIDs(db *SQLiteStorage) error {
    // Read all issues
    issues, err := db.ListIssues(ctx, ListOptions{Status: "all"})
    
    // Generate mapping: old ID → new hash ID
    mapping := make(map[string]string)
    for _, issue := range issues {
        hashID := GenerateHashID(
            issue.Title,
            issue.Description,
            issue.CreatedAt,
            db.workspaceID,
        )
        mapping[issue.ID] = hashID
    }
    
    // Detect hash collisions (extremely rare)
    if hasCollisions(mapping) {
        return fmt.Errorf("hash collision detected, aborting")
    }
    
    return nil
}
```

### 3. Update Database Schema
```sql
-- Add alias column
ALTER TABLE issues ADD COLUMN alias INTEGER UNIQUE;

-- Populate aliases from old IDs
UPDATE issues SET alias = CAST(SUBSTR(id, 4) AS INTEGER)
  WHERE id LIKE 'bd-%' AND SUBSTR(id, 4) GLOB '[0-9]*';

-- Create new issues_new table with hash IDs
CREATE TABLE issues_new (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,  -- Hash IDs now
    alias INTEGER UNIQUE,
    title TEXT NOT NULL,
    -- ... rest of schema
);

-- Copy data with ID mapping
INSERT INTO issues_new SELECT 
    <new_hash_id>,  -- From mapping
    alias,
    title,
    -- ...
FROM issues;

-- Drop old table, rename new
DROP TABLE issues;
ALTER TABLE issues_new RENAME TO issues;
```

### 4. Update Dependencies
```sql
-- Update depends_on_id using mapping
UPDATE dependencies 
SET issue_id = <new_hash_id>,
    depends_on_id = <new_depends_on_hash_id>
FROM mapping;
```

### 5. Update Text References
```go
// Update all text fields that mention old IDs
func updateTextReferences(db *SQLiteStorage, mapping map[string]string) error {
    for oldID, newID := range mapping {
        // Update description, notes, design, acceptance_criteria
        db.Exec(`UPDATE issues SET 
            description = REPLACE(description, ?, ?),
            notes = REPLACE(notes, ?, ?),
            design = REPLACE(design, ?, ?),
            acceptance_criteria = REPLACE(acceptance_criteria, ?, ?)
        `, oldID, newID, oldID, newID, oldID, newID, oldID, newID)
    }
}
```

### 6. Export to JSONL
```bash
bd export  # Writes hash IDs to .beads/issues.jsonl
git add .beads/issues.jsonl
git commit -m "Migrate to hash-based IDs"
```

## Output
```bash
$ bd migrate --hash-ids
Migrating to hash-based IDs...
✓ Backup created: .beads/beads.db.backup-1730246400
✓ Generated 150 hash IDs
✓ No hash collisions detected
✓ Updated issues table schema
✓ Updated 150 issue IDs
✓ Updated 87 dependencies
✓ Updated 234 text references
✓ Exported to .beads/issues.jsonl
✓ Migration complete!

Next steps:
  1. Test: bd list, bd show #1, etc.
  2. Commit: git commit -m "Migrate to hash-based IDs"
  3. Push: git push origin main
  4. Notify collaborators to pull and re-init
```

## Dry Run Mode
```bash
$ bd migrate --hash-ids --dry-run
[DRY RUN] Would migrate 150 issues:
  bd-1c63eb84   → bd-af78e9a2  (alias: #1)
  bd-9063acda   → bd-e5f6a7b8  (alias: #2)
  ...
  bd-150 → bd-9a8b7c6d  (alias: #150)

[DRY RUN] Would update:
  - 150 issue IDs
  - 87 dependencies
  - 234 text references in descriptions/notes

No changes made. Run without --dry-run to apply.
```

## Files to Create

- internal/storage/sqlite/migrations/hash_ids.go

## Testing

- Test migration on large database (1000 issues)

- Test migrated database works correctly

mv .beads/beads.db.backup-1234567890 .beads/beads.db

```

## Multi-Clone Coordination
**Important**: All clones must migrate before syncing:

1. Coordinator sends message: "Migrating to hash IDs on 2025-10-30 at 10:00 UTC"

description:
Update all CLI commands to accept both hash IDs (bd-af78e9a2) and aliases (#42, or just 42).

## Parsing Logic
```go
// internal/utils/id_parser.go
func ParseIssueID(input string) (issueID string, err error) {
    // Hash ID: bd-af78e9a2
    if strings.HasPrefix(input, "bd-") {
        return input, nil
    }
    
    // Alias: #42 or 42
    aliasStr := strings.TrimPrefix(input, "#")
    alias, err := strconv.Atoi(aliasStr)
    if err != nil {
        return "", fmt.Errorf("invalid issue ID: %s", input)
    }
    
    // Resolve alias to hash ID
    return storage.GetIssueIDByAlias(alias)
}
```

## Commands to Update
All commands that accept issue IDs:

### 1. bd show
```bash
bd show bd-af78e9a2        # Hash ID
bd show #42                # Alias
bd show 42                 # Alias (shorthand)
bd show bd-af78e9a2 #42    # Mixed (multiple IDs)
```

### 2. bd update
```bash
bd update #42 --status in_progress
bd update bd-af78e9a2 --priority 1
```

### 3. bd close
```bash
bd close #42 --reason "Done"
```

### 4. bd dep add/tree
```bash
bd dep add #42 #1 --type blocks
bd dep tree bd-af78e9a2
```

### 5. bd label add/remove
```bash
bd label add #42 critical
```

### 6. bd merge
```bash
bd merge #42 #43 --into #41
```

## Display Format
Default to showing aliases in output:
```bash
$ bd list
#1   Fix authentication bug          P1  open
#2   Add logging to daemon           P2  open  
#42  Investigate jujutsu integration P3  open
```

With `--format=hash` flag:
```bash
$ bd list --format=hash
bd-af78e9a2  Fix authentication bug          P1  open
bd-e5f6a7b8  Add logging to daemon           P2  open
bd-1a2b3c4d  Investigate jujutsu integration P3  open
```

## Files to Modify
- internal/utils/id_parser.go (new)
- cmd/bd/show.go
- cmd/bd/update.go
- cmd/bd/close.go
- cmd/bd/reopen.go
- cmd/bd/dep.go
- cmd/bd/label.go
- cmd/bd/merge.go
- cmd/bd/list.go (add --format flag)

## Testing
- Test hash ID parsing
- Test alias parsing (#42, 42)
- Test mixed IDs in single command
- Test error on invalid ID
- Test alias resolution failure
title: CLI accepts both hash IDs and aliases

description:
Update JSONL import/export to use hash IDs, store aliases separately.

## Current JSONL Format
```jsonl
{"id":"bd-1c63eb84","title":"Fix bug","status":"open",...}
{"id":"bd-9063acda","title":"Add test","status":"open",...}
```

## New JSONL Format (Option A: Include Alias)
```jsonl
{"id":"bd-af78e9a2","alias":1,"title":"Fix bug","status":"open",...}
{"id":"bd-e5f6a7b8","alias":2,"title":"Add test","status":"open",...}
```

## New JSONL Format (Option B: Hash ID Only)
```jsonl
{"id":"bd-af78e9a2","title":"Fix bug","status":"open",...}
{"id":"bd-e5f6a7b8","title":"Add test","status":"open",...}
```

Store aliases in separate .beads/aliases.jsonl (local only, git-ignored):
```jsonl
{"hash":"bd-af78e9a2","alias":1}
{"hash":"bd-e5f6a7b8","alias":2}
```

**Recommendation**: Option B (hash only in main JSONL)
- Cleaner git diffs (no alias conflicts)
- Aliases are workspace-local preference
- Main JSONL is canonical, portable

## Export Changes
File: cmd/bd/export.go
```go
// Export issues with hash IDs
for _, issue := range issues {
    json := marshalIssue(issue)  // Uses issue.ID (hash)
    // Don't include alias in JSONL
}

// Separately export aliases to .beads/aliases.jsonl
exportAliases(issues)
```

## Import Changes  
File: cmd/bd/import.go, internal/importer/importer.go
```go
// Import issues by hash ID
issue := unmarshalIssue(line)
// Assign new alias on import (don't use incoming alias)
issue.Alias = getNextAlias()

// No collision detection needed! Hash IDs are globally unique
```

## Dependency Reference Format
No change needed - already uses issue IDs:
```json
{"depends_on_id":"bd-af78e9a2","type":"blocks"}
```

## Files to Modify
- cmd/bd/export.go (use hash IDs)
- cmd/bd/import.go (import hash IDs, assign aliases)
- internal/importer/importer.go (remove collision detection!)
- .gitignore (add .beads/aliases.jsonl)

## Testing
- Test export produces hash IDs
- Test import assigns new aliases
- Test dependencies preserved with hash IDs
- Test no collision detection triggered
title: Update JSONL format to use hash IDs
Implement hash ID generation in CreateIssue

description:
Add bd swarm create to create a swarm molecule from an epic.

## Context
Swarms ARE persistent molecules - not separate JSON state. This command creates
a swarm molecule that:
- Links to the epic it orchestrates
- Has mol_type=swarm
- Specifies a coordinator
- Can be discovered by any agent

## Core Principle: Discovery Over Tracking
The swarm molecule doesn't track step-by-step state. Instead:
- Query bd ready --parent=<epic> to find the ready front
- Query bd list --parent=<epic> --status=closed to find completed
- Derive progress from children's statuses

## Implementation
1. New command: bd swarm create <epic-id> [--coordinator=<addr>]
2. Validate epic structure first (call bd swarm validate)
3. Create molecule bead:
   - mol_type: swarm
   - epic: <epic-id>
   - coordinator: <addr>
   - status: open
4. Return the swarm molecule ID

## Single-Issue Wrapping
If given a single issue (not epic), auto-wrap:
- Create an epic with that issue as child
- Then create swarm molecule

## Reference
~/gt/docs/swarm-architecture.md - 'Swarm Structure' section

## Acceptance
- bd swarm create <epic> creates a swarm molecule
- Molecule has mol_type=swarm
- Links to epic via epic field
- Validates structure before creation
title: bd swarm create command

description:
The prefix detection logic in bd import incorrectly identifies issues like 'vc-baseline-test' and 'vc-92cl-gate-test' as having different prefixes ('vc-baseline-' and 'vc-92cl-gate-') instead of recognizing them as having the standard 'vc-' prefix with hyphenated suffixes.

This breaks import with error: 'prefix mismatch detected: database uses vc- but found issues with prefixes: [vc-92cl-gate- (1 issues) vc-baseline- (1 issues)]'

The prefix should be determined by the pattern: prefix is everything up to and including the first hyphen. The suffix can contain hyphens without being treated as part of the prefix.

Example problematic IDs:
- vc-baseline-test (detected as prefix: vc-baseline-)
- vc-92cl-gate-test (detected as prefix: vc-92cl-gate-)
- vc-test (correctly detected as prefix: vc-)

Impact: Users cannot use descriptive multi-part IDs without triggering false prefix mismatch errors.
title: Prefix detection treats embedded hyphens as prefix delimiters

description:
When `bd gate check` encounters a gh:run gate with a non-numeric await_id (workflow name hint like 'release.yml'), it currently returns:

```
awaiting discovery (workflow hint: release.yml) - run 'bd gate discover'
```

This requires manual intervention. The fix committed in 8528fcaa added the message, but didn't add auto-discovery.

**Proposal:** `checkGHRun` should automatically call the discovery logic when it encounters a workflow name hint, rather than requiring a separate command.

**Why this matters:**
- The beads-release formula creates gates with `id = 'release.yml'`
- Agents running the formula shouldn't need to know about the discover command
- The chicken-and-egg problem: you can't release the version that fixes the release process

**Implementation:**
1. In `checkGHRun`, when `!isNumericID(gate.AwaitID)`:
   - Query recent GitHub runs
   - Match using `workflowNameMatches`
   - Auto-update the gate's await_id
   - Then proceed with the normal check
title: Gate check should auto-discover non-numeric await_id
Test parent task

description:
Create an npm package that wraps native bd binaries for easy installation in Claude Code for Web and other Node.js environments.

## Problem
Claude Code for Web sandboxes are full Linux VMs with npm support, but cannot easily download binaries from GitHub releases due to network restrictions or tooling limitations.

## Solution
Publish bd as an npm package that:
- Downloads platform-specific native binaries during postinstall
- Provides a CLI wrapper that invokes the native binary
- Works seamlessly in Claude Code for Web SessionStart hooks
- Maintains full feature parity (uses native SQLite)

## Benefits vs WASM
- ✅ Full SQLite support (no custom VFS needed)
- ✅ All features work identically to native bd
- ✅ Better performance (native vs WASM overhead)
- ✅ ~4 hours effort vs ~2 days for WASM
- ✅ Minimal maintenance burden

## Success Criteria
- npm install @beads/bd works in Claude Code for Web
- All bd commands function identically to native binary
- SessionStart hook documented for auto-installation
- Package published to npm registry
title: npm package for bd with native binaries

description:
During swarm bd-784c, polecats (Toast, Nux) were spawned without --dangerously-skip-permissions or equivalent auto-accept mode.

**Problem:**
Every edit, bash command, and tool use required manual confirmation via tmux send-keys. This defeats the purpose of autonomous polecat operation.

**Expected:**
Polecats in a swarm should run with permissions bypassed so they can work autonomously.

**Workaround used:**
- Manually sent Enter keys via tmux to accept prompts
- Eventually polecats entered 'bypass permissions on' mode after restart

**Suggestion:**
- gt sling should pass --dangerously-skip-permissions by default for polecats
- Or polecats should have a .claude/settings.local.json pre-configured for auto-accept
title: Polecats should spawn with auto-accept mode enabled

description:
**Problem:**
`bd hook` without `--agent` defaults to `$USER` (e.g., "stevey") instead of detecting the agent identity from the current working directory.

**Expected behavior:**
When running from `/Users/stevey/gt/beads/crew/dave`, the agent should be detected as `beads/crew/dave`.

**Current behavior:**
```bash
$ pwd
/Users/stevey/gt/beads/crew/dave
$ bd hook
Hook: stevey
  (empty)

$ bd hook --agent beads/crew/dave
Hook: beads/crew/dave
  📌 bd-hobo (mol) - open
```

**Fix:**
bd hook should use the same cwd-based agent detection that other commands use (similar to how `gt mail` determines identity from cwd).
title: bd hook: detect agent from cwd instead of defaulting to $USER

description:
After running bd migrate-tombstones and removing deletions.jsonl from git, the file gets recreated locally on subsequent bd commands.

**Steps to reproduce:**
1. Run bd migrate-tombstones (converts to inline tombstones)
2. Remove deletions.jsonl from git and filesystem
3. Run bd sync or bd stats
4. deletions.jsonl reappears

**Expected behavior:**
After migration, deletions.jsonl should not be recreated. All tombstone data should come from inline tombstones in issues.jsonl.

**Workaround:** Add deletions.jsonl to .gitignore to prevent re-tracking. File still gets created but won't pollute the repo.
title: deletions.jsonl recreated locally after tombstone migration

## GitHub Issue
https://github.com/steveyegge/beads/issues/1015

## Problem
The `remoteSyncTicker` periodic sync never actually pulls from remote because `performAutoImport()` has a `hasJSONLChanged()` check that returns early BEFORE the git pull can execute.

## Root Cause
In `cmd/bd/daemon_sync.go`, `performAutoImport()` checks the hash BEFORE pulling:
- Check compares local JSONL to database hash
- Since no git pull happened, local file hasn't changed
- Returns early, never reaches `syncBranchPull()`

## Fix
Move `hasJSONLChanged()` check to AFTER `syncBranchPull()`:
1. Pull from git first
2. Then check if pulled JSONL differs from DB
3. If changed, proceed with import

## Files
- `cmd/bd/daemon_sync.go` - `performAutoImport()` function
title: Fix periodic auto-pull never executing git pull (GH#1015)

description:
Add hook system to bd close for notifications and custom actions.

## Scope (MVP)

Implement **command hooks only** for bd close. Deferred: notify, webhook types.

## Implementation

### 1. Config Schema

Add to internal/configfile/config.go:

```go
type HooksConfig struct {
    OnClose []HookEntry `yaml:"on_close,omitempty"`
}

type HookEntry struct {
    Command string `yaml:"command"`           // Shell command to run
    Name    string `yaml:"name,omitempty"`    // Optional display name
}
```

Add `Hooks HooksConfig` field to Config struct.

### 2. Hook Execution

Create internal/hooks/close_hooks.go:

```go
func RunCloseHooks(ctx context.Context, cfg *configfile.Config, issue *types.Issue) error {
    for _, hook := range cfg.Hooks.OnClose {
        cmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, "sh", "-c", hook.Command)
        cmd.Env = append(os.Environ(),
            "BEAD_ID="+issue.ID,
            "BEAD_TITLE="+issue.Title,
            "BEAD_TYPE="+string(issue.IssueType),
            "BEAD_PRIORITY="+strconv.Itoa(issue.Priority),
            "BEAD_CLOSE_REASON="+issue.CloseReason,
        )
        cmd.Stdout = os.Stdout
        cmd.Stderr = os.Stderr
        if err := cmd.Run(); err \!= nil {
            // Log warning but dont fail the close
            fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "Warning: close hook %q failed: %v\n", hook.Name, err)
        }
    }
    return nil
}
```

### 3. Integration Point

In cmd/bd/close.go, after successful close:

```go
// Run close hooks
if cfg := configfile.Load(); cfg \!= nil {
    hooks.RunCloseHooks(ctx, cfg, closedIssue)
}
```

### 4. Example Config

```yaml
# .beads/config.yaml
hooks:
  on_close:
    - name: show-next
      command: bd ready --limit 1
    - name: context-check  
      command: echo "Issue $BEAD_ID closed. Check context if nearing limit."
```

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| BEAD_ID | Issue ID (e.g., bd-abc1) |
| BEAD_TITLE | Issue title |
| BEAD_TYPE | Issue type (task, bug, feature, etc.) |
| BEAD_PRIORITY | Priority (0-4) |
| BEAD_CLOSE_REASON | Close reason if provided |

## Testing

Add test in internal/hooks/close_hooks_test.go:
- Test hook execution with mock config
- Test env vars are set correctly
- Test hook failure doesnt block close

## Files to Create/Modify

1. **Create:** internal/hooks/close_hooks.go
2. **Create:** internal/hooks/close_hooks_test.go  
3. **Modify:** internal/configfile/config.go (add HooksConfig)
4. **Modify:** cmd/bd/close.go (call RunCloseHooks)
5. **Modify:** docs/CONFIG.md (document hooks config)

## Out of Scope (Future)

- notify hook type (gt mail integration)
- webhook type (HTTP POST)
- on_create, on_update hooks
- Hook timeout configuration
- Parallel hook execution
title: bd close hooks: context check and notifications

description:
## Agent Beads Need Queryable Labels

Currently agent beads have role_type/rig in description text, not as labels. This breaks @group resolution in gt mail.

## Current State

```json
{
  "id": "gt-gastown-witness",
  "issue_type": "agent",
  "description": "...\\nrole_type: witness\\nrig: gastown\\n..."
}
```

Cannot query: `bd list --type=agent --label=role_type:witness` returns nothing.

## Required

Agent bead creation should add labels:
- `role_type:<type>` (witness, refinery, crew, polecat, dog, mayor, deacon)
- `rig:<rig>` (gastown, beads, or "town" for town-level)

## Where to Fix

gt polecat/crew/agent creation commands should add labels:
```bash
bd create --type=agent ... --labels="role_type:witness,rig:gastown"
```

## Queries Enabled

```bash
bd list --type=agent --label=role_type:witness    # All witnesses
bd list --type=agent --label=rig:gastown          # All gastown agents
bd list --type=agent --label=role_type:dog        # All dogs
```

## Acceptance
- New agent beads created with role_type/rig labels
- Existing agent beads backfilled (one-time script)
- @group patterns work in gt mail router
title: Agent beads: structured labels for filtering

description:
## Problem

The importer still has rename detection (importer.go:482-500) that triggers when same content hash has different IDs. With hash IDs, this shouldn't happen, but when it does (test data, bugs, legacy data), the import fails:

```
failed to handle rename bd-ce75 -> bd-5a90: rename collision handling removed - should not occur with hash IDs
```

## Current Behavior

1. Importer finds same content hash with different IDs
2. Calls handleRename() (line 490)
3. handleRename() errors out (line 294): "rename collision handling removed"
4. Import fails

## Expected Behavior

Same content hash + different IDs should be treated as an **update**, not a rename:
- Keep existing ID (already in database)
- Update fields if incoming has newer timestamp
- Discard incoming ID (it's wrong - hash should have generated same ID)

## Impact

- Import fails on legitimate edge cases (test data, data corruption)
- Cryptic error message
- Blocks sync operations

## Fix

In handleRename() or import loop, instead of erroring:
```go
// Same content, different ID - treat as update
if incoming.UpdatedAt.After(existing.UpdatedAt) {
    existing.Status = incoming.Status
    // ... copy other fields
    s.UpdateIssue(ctx, existing)
}
result.Updated++
```

## Files
- internal/importer/importer.go:271-294 (handleRename)
- internal/importer/importer.go:482-500 (rename detection)

## Repro
Import JSONL with bd-ce75 and bd-5a90 (both "Test parent issue" but different content hashes).
title: Import fails on same-content-different-ID instead of treating as update
Improve Beads test coverage from 46% to 80%

description:
When running bd admin compact --prune --older-than=0, the output says 'No expired tombstones to prune (TTL: 30 days)' - the flag value is ignored.

Root cause: In runCompactPrune() in compact_tombstone.go:180, the check 'if compactOlderThan > 0' means that --older-than=0 is treated the same as not passing the flag, defaulting to 30 days.

Fix: Change the default flag value in compact.go:785 to -1 (sentinel for 'use default'), and treat 0 as 'expire all tombstones' by passing a negative TTL to pruneExpiredTombstones().

Moved from gt-65gwa (gastown) - was filed in wrong rig.
title: bd admin compact --older-than=0 ignores the flag, uses default 30 days

description:
## Problem

Running `go test ./internal/importer/... -v` takes an unacceptably long time (minutes). For a project this size, tests should complete in seconds.

## Impact

- Slows down development iteration
- AI agents waste time waiting for tests
- Blocks rapid bug fixes and validation
- Poor developer experience

## Investigation Needed

- Profile which tests are slow
- Check for unnecessary sleeps, timeouts, or integration tests
- Look for tests that could be parallelized
- Consider splitting unit vs integration tests

## Goal

Test suite for a single package should complete in <5 seconds, ideally <2 seconds.
notes:
## Optimizations Applied

1. **Added t.Parallel() to CLI tests** (13 tests) - allows concurrent execution
2. **Removed unnecessary 200ms sleep** in daemon_autoimport_test.go - Execute() forces auto-import synchronously
3. **Reduced filesystem settle wait** from 100ms → 50ms on non-Windows platforms
4. **Optimized debouncer test sleeps** (9 reductions):
   - Before debounce waits: 30ms → 20ms, 20ms → 10ms
   - After debounce waits: 40ms → 35ms, 30ms → 35ms, etc.
   - Thread safety test: 100ms → 70ms
   - Sequential cycles: 50ms → 40ms (3x)
   - Cancel tests: 70-80ms → 60ms

## Results

### cmd/bd package (main improvement target):
- **Before**: 5+ minutes (timeout)
- **After**: ~18-20 seconds
- **Speedup**: ~15-18x faster

### internal/importer package:
- **After**: <1 second (0.9s)

### Full test suite (with `-short` flag):
- Most packages complete in <2s
- Total runtime constrained by sequential integration tests

## Known Issues

- TestConcurrentExternalRefImports hangs due to :memory: connection pool issue (bd-b121)
- Some sync_branch tests may need sequential execution (git worktree conflicts)
title: Tests take too long - unacceptable for project size

description:
## Problem

When creating a gate with `--await` fields (e.g., `gh:run:123`), the await_type and await_id are initially stored correctly in the SQLite database. However, they get cleared (set to empty strings) during subsequent bd commands due to the auto-flush/auto-import cycle.

## Reproduction Steps

1. Create a gate with await:
   ```
   bd gate create --molecule test-mol --await gh:run:20517738002
   ```
   Output shows correct `await_type: gh:run, await_id: 20517738002`

2. Check database - fields are present

3. Run any bd command (e.g., `bd list`)

4. Check database again - await_type and await_id are now empty

## Root Cause

Gates are wisps (ephemeral issues). During the auto-flush cycle:
1. Auto-flush exports issues to JSONL (filtering out wisps)
2. Auto-import reads JSONL and updates DB rows
3. The update clears fields that weren't in the JSONL (including await fields)

## Workaround

Using `--no-auto-import --no-auto-flush` flags preserves the fields, but this isn't practical for normal use.

## Impact

GitHub gate evaluation (`bd gate eval`) cannot work because by the time eval runs, the await fields have been cleared.

## Suggested Fix

Either:
1. Don't clear await fields during auto-import if they weren't in the source
2. Store wisp fields separately from the main JSONL export
3. Include wisps in export but filter them at sync time
title: Gate await fields cleared by auto-flush/auto-import cycle

description:
## Problem

`gitCommitBeadsDir` in `cmd/bd/sync.go` runs `git add .beads/` which stages all files in the directory, including snapshot files that are listed in `.beads/.gitignore`.

If a snapshot file (e.g., `beads.left.meta.json`) was ever committed before being added to `.gitignore`, git continues to track it. This causes merge conflicts when multiple polecats run `bd sync` concurrently, since each one modifies and commits these temporary files.

## Root Cause

Line ~568 in sync.go:
```go
addCmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, "git", "add", beadsDir)
```

This stages everything in `.beads/`, but `.gitignore` only prevents *untracked* files from being added - it doesn't affect already-tracked files.

## Suggested Fix

Option A: After `git add .beads/`, run `git reset` on snapshot files:
```go
exec.Command("git", "reset", "HEAD", ".beads/beads.*.jsonl", ".beads/*.meta.json")
```

Option B: Stage only specific files instead of the whole directory:
```go
exec.Command("git", "add", ".beads/issues.jsonl", ".beads/deletions.jsonl", ".beads/metadata.json")
```

Option C: Detect and untrack snapshot files if they're tracked:
```go
// Check if file is tracked: git ls-files --error-unmatch <file>
// If tracked, run: git rm --cached <file>
```

Option B is probably cleanest - explicitly add only the files that should be committed.
title: bd sync should not stage gitignored snapshot files

description:
## Problem

`gt rig shutdown wyvern` updates runtime JSON to `state: stopped` but does NOT kill the tmux sessions (`gt-wyvern-witness`, `gt-wyvern-refinery`).

Since `gt rig status` checks for tmux sessions (correctly), it shows them as running.

## Evidence

After `gt rig shutdown wyvern`:
- Runtime JSON: `state: stopped` ✓
- tmux sessions: still exist ✗

## Impact

- Rig appears running after shutdown
- tmux status shows green icon for 'stopped' rigs
- User confusion

## Root Cause

`gt rig shutdown` in internal/cmd/rig.go updates runtime state but doesn't call `tmux kill-session` for witness/refinery.

## Fix

In shutdown logic, after setting state to stopped, kill:
- `gt-{rig}-witness` session
- `gt-{rig}-refinery` session
title: BUG: gt rig shutdown doesn't kill tmux sessions

description:
## Context
GH#604 raised concerns about bd onboard bloating AGENTS.md with ~100+ lines of static instructions that:
- Load every session whether beads is being used or not
- Get stale when bd upgrades
- Waste tokens

## Solution
Simplify `bd onboard` to output a minimal snippet (~2 lines) that points to `bd prime`:

```markdown
## Issue Tracking
This project uses beads (`bd`) for issue tracking.
Run `bd prime` for workflow context, or hooks auto-inject it.
```

## Rationale
- `bd prime` is dynamic, concise (~80 lines), and always matches installed bd version
- Hooks already auto-inject `bd prime` at session start when .beads/ detected
- AGENTS.md only needs to mention beads exists, not contain full instructions

## Implementation
1. Update `cmd/bd/onboard.go` to output minimal snippet
2. Keep `--output` flag for BD_GUIDE.md generation (may still be useful)
3. Update help text to explain the new approach
title: Simplify bd onboard to minimal AGENTS.md snippet pointing to bd prime

description:
GitHub issue #278 reports that the daemon exits after <=5 seconds on macOS because it incorrectly treats PID 1 (launchd) as a dead parent.

When the daemon detaches on macOS, it gets reparented to PID 1 (launchd), which is the init process. The checkParentProcessAlive function was incorrectly treating PID 1 as a sign that the parent died.

Fixed by changing the logic to treat PID 1 as a valid parent for detached daemons.
title: Fix daemon exiting after 5s on macOS due to PID 1 parent monitoring

description:
## Problem

`bd cleanup` deletes closed issues (converting them to tombstones) but does NOT prune expired tombstones. Users expect 'cleanup' to do comprehensive cleanup.

## Current Behavior

1. `bd cleanup --force` → converts closed issues to tombstones
2. Expired tombstones (>30 days) remain in issues.jsonl
3. User must separately run `bd compact` to prune tombstones
4. `bd doctor` warns about expired tombstones: 'Run bd compact to prune'

## Expected Behavior

`bd cleanup` should also prune expired tombstones from issues.jsonl.

## Impact

With v0.30.0 making tombstones the default migration path, this UX gap becomes more visible. Users cleaning up their database shouldn't need to know about a separate `bd compact` command.

## Proposed Solution

Call `pruneExpiredTombstones()` at the end of the cleanup command (same function used by compact). Report results:

```
✓ Deleted 15 closed issue(s)
✓ Pruned 3 expired tombstone(s) (older than 30 days)
```

## Files to Modify

- `cmd/bd/cleanup.go` - Add call to pruneExpiredTombstones after deleteBatch
- May need to move pruneExpiredTombstones to shared location if not already accessible

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `bd cleanup --force` prunes expired tombstones after deleting closed issues
- [ ] `bd cleanup --dry-run` shows what tombstones would be pruned
- [ ] JSON output includes tombstone prune results
title: bd cleanup should also prune expired tombstones

description:
## Context

Implemented the molecular chemistry UX commands per the design docs:
- gastown/mayor/rig/docs/molecular-chemistry.md
- gastown/mayor/rig/docs/chemistry-design-changes.md

Commit: cadf798b

## New Commands to Test

| Command | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `bd pour <proto>` | Instantiate proto as persistent mol |
| `bd wisp create <proto>` | Instantiate proto as ephemeral wisp |
| `bd hook [--agent]` | Inspect what's on an agent's hook |

## Enhanced Commands to Test

| Command | Changes |
|---------|---------|
| `bd mol spawn --pour` | New flag, `--persistent` deprecated |
| `bd mol bond --pour` | Force liquid phase on wisp target |
| `bd pin --for <agent> --start` | Chemistry workflow support |

## Test Scenarios

description:
## Problem

`bd slot set` fails when setting a slot to a bead that lives in a different beads database (cross-db routing).

## Evidence

When spawning a polecat:
```
Warning: could not set role slot: bd slot set gt-gastown-polecat-chumbucket role hq-polecat-role: 
Error: failed to resolve bead hq-polecat-role: no issue found matching "hq-polecat-role"
```

The agent bead `gt-gastown-polecat-chumbucket` is in rig beads (`gastown/mayor/rig/.beads/`).
The role bead `hq-polecat-role` is in town beads (`~/gt/.beads/`).

`bd show hq-polecat-role` works fine (uses prefix routing via routes.jsonl).
`bd slot set ... hq-polecat-role` fails (does not use routing).

## Root Cause

`bd slot set` resolves the target bead in the current database only, ignoring the `hq-` prefix that should route to town beads.

## Expected Behavior

`bd slot set <agent> <slot> <bead>` should:
1. Check if `<bead>` has a routable prefix (e.g., `hq-`, `gt-`, `bd-`)
2. If so, resolve it via routes.jsonl like `bd show` does
3. Store the bead reference (cross-db references are valid for slots)

## Workaround

Currently the warning is non-fatal - the role slot just is not set. But this breaks role-based queries.

## Files

- `cmd/slot.go` - slot set command implementation
- Need to add routing logic similar to `cmd/show.go`
title: bd slot set doesn't support cross-beads prefix routing

description:
## Problem
Getting a full swarm status requires running 6+ commands:
- `town list <rig>` for each rig
- `town mail inbox` as Boss
- `bd list --status=open/in_progress` per rig

This is slow and error-prone for both humans and agents.

## Proposed Solution
Add `town report [RIG]` command that aggregates:
- All rigs with polecat states (running/stopped, awake/asleep)
- Boss inbox summary (unread count, recent senders)
- Aggregate issue counts per rig (open/in_progress/blocked)

Example output:
```
=== beads ===
Polecats: 5 (5 running, 0 stopped)
Issues: 20 open, 0 in_progress, 0 blocked

=== gastown ===
Polecats: 6 (4 running, 2 stopped)
Issues: 0 open, 0 in_progress, 0 blocked

=== Boss Mail ===
Unread: 10 | Total: 22
Recent: rictus (21:19), scrotus (21:14), immortanjoe (21:14)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `town report` shows all rigs
- [ ] `town report <rig>` shows single rig detail
- [ ] Output is concise and scannable
- [ ] Completes in <2 seconds
title: Add 'town report' command for aggregated swarm status

description:
Template/workflow entities should have visually distinct prefixes from regular issues.

**Problem:**
- Protos (bd-7bs4) look like regular issues - invites squashing
- Molecules (poured instances) also use bd- prefix
- Wisps are in separate DB but still use bd- when referenced

**Proposed Prefixes:**
- `proto-` for templates (e.g., proto-release, proto-review)
- `mol-` for active molecules (poured from protos)
- `wisp-` for ephemeral wisps (vapor phase)

**Benefits:**
- Instant visual recognition of entity type
- Prevents accidental modification of templates
- Clear lifecycle: proto → mol → wisp → digest

**Implementation options:**
1. Separate databases with different prefixes
2. Issue type determines prefix generation
3. Naming convention enforced by bd pour/wisp commands
title: Distinct prefixes for protos, molecules, wisps

## Problem

gt sling <formula-name> doesn't find formulas from the registry that bd formula list shows.

   - Problem: --formula flag doesn't exist

2. gt sling beads-release beads/crew/emma --var version=0.42.0
   - Error: open .../beads-release: no such file or directory
   - Problem: Looks for file in cwd, not formula registry

## What worked

bd mol wisp beads-release --var version=0.42.0 - correctly finds formula from .beads/formulas/

## Expected

gt sling should use the same formula lookup as bd mol wisp - checking .beads/formulas/*.formula.json

title: gt sling should look up formulas from .beads/formulas/ registry

description:
When cooking a formula with `needs` fields on steps, the dependencies are not created between sibling steps.

## Expected

Formula:
```yaml
steps:
  - id: inbox-check
    title: Check inbox
  - id: trigger-spawns
    title: Nudge polecats
    needs: [inbox-check]
```

Should create:
- mol-foo.inbox-check (no deps)
- mol-foo.trigger-spawns → depends on → mol-foo.inbox-check

## Actual

Both steps only depend on the parent proto:
- mol-foo.inbox-check → depends on → mol-foo
- mol-foo.trigger-spawns → depends on → mol-foo

The `needs` field is ignored.

## Impact

This breaks the step execution order. Steps that should wait for predecessors will run in parallel or out of order.

## Reproduction

```bash
bd cook mol-deacon-patrol.formula.yaml
bd show mol-deacon-patrol.trigger-pending-spawns
# Shows: Depends on mol-deacon-patrol (parent only)
# Should show: Depends on mol-deacon-patrol.inbox-check
```
title: bd cook: needs field not converted to step dependencies
Updated title

description:
## Problem
When a bd daemon crashes or is killed without cleanup, it leaves a stale daemon.lock file. Subsequent bd commands try to connect to the dead daemon, causing 5+ second timeouts before falling back to direct mode.

Found during gt status optimization (gt-zdtpw) - one stale wyvern daemon was causing 10+ second delays.

## Evidence
```
# Lock files with stale PIDs:
$ find ~/gt -name "daemon.lock" -exec cat {} \;
{"pid": 11045, ...}  # Not running (from Dec 27)
{"pid": 15986, ...}  # Not running (from Dec 27)
{"pid": 98055, ...}  # Just restarted

# But running daemons have different PIDs:
$ ps aux | grep "bd.*daemon"
11847, 11683, 11624  # None match the lock files!
```

## Proposed fixes

### 1. Validate lock on startup (quick fix)
Before trusting daemon.lock, check if the PID is still running:
```python
def is_daemon_alive(lock_path):
    lock = json.load(open(lock_path))
    try:
        os.kill(lock["pid"], 0)  # Signal 0 = check if alive
        return True
    except OSError:
        return False
```

### 2. Heartbeat mechanism (robust fix)
Daemon writes periodic heartbeat to lock file or separate file.
Clients check heartbeat age before attempting IPC.

### 3. Lock cleanup in daemon startup
When starting a new daemon, always check for and clean stale locks.

### 4. Graceful shutdown handling
Register signal handlers (SIGTERM, SIGINT) to clean up lock on exit.

## Workaround
For now, gastown uses `--no-daemon` for all read operations to avoid this issue.
title: Stale daemon.lock files cause 5+ second delays

description:
Implement molecule execution state tracking.

## State Location

```
.beads/molecules/<mol-id>.state.yaml
```

## State Schema

```yaml
id: mol-deacon-patrol
formula: mol-deacon-patrol  # Source formula
bonded_at: ISO timestamp
bonded_by: entity who created it

# Execution state
status: running | paused | completed | failed
current_step: step-id
started_at: ISO timestamp
completed_at: ISO timestamp (if done)

# Loop tracking
reset_count: 0
last_reset_at: null

# Per-step state
steps:
  inbox-check:
    status: completed | in_progress | pending
    started_at: ...
    completed_at: ...
  spawn-work:
    status: pending
  self-inspect:
    status: pending

# Variables (from formula instantiation)
variables:
  rig: gastown
  issue_id: gt-xxx
```

## Operations

- CreateState(molID, formula, variables) - Initialize state
- LoadState(molID) - Read current state  
- SaveState(molID, state) - Write state
- AdvanceStep(molID) - Mark current complete, find next
- ResetState(molID) - Reset all steps to pending

## Files

- internal/mol/state.go
- internal/mol/types.go
title: Molecule execution state management

description:
## Problem

When working with multiple beads workspaces (clones) sharing the same git remote, deleted issues keep coming back.

## Reproduction

1. Clone A deletes issue `bd-xyz` via `bd delete bd-xyz --force`
2. Clone A daemon syncs and pushes to GitHub
3. Clone B still has `bd-xyz` in its database
4. Clone B daemon exports and pushes its JSONL
5. Clone A pulls and imports → `bd-xyz` comes back!

## Root Cause

**No deletion tracking mechanism.** The system has no way to distinguish between:
- "Issue doesn't exist in JSONL because it was deleted" 
- "Issue doesn't exist in JSONL because the export is stale"

Import treats missing issues as "not in this export" rather than "explicitly deleted."

## Solution Options

1. **Tombstone records** - Keep deleted issues in JSONL with `"status":"deleted"` or `"deleted_at"` field
2. **Deletion log** - Separate `.beads/deletions.jsonl` file tracking all deleted IDs
3. **Three-way merge** - Import compares: DB state, old JSONL, new JSONL
4. **Manual conflict resolution** - Detect resurrection and prompt user

## Related

- Similar to resurrection logic for orphaned children (bd-cc4f)
- beads-merge tool handles this better with 3-way merge
title: Deletions not propagated across multi-workspace sync

description:
## Summary

Replace tool-specific setup commands (cursor, claude, etc.) with a generic recipe-based system. New tools become config entries, not code changes.

## Motivation

GH#898 requests Kilo Code integration. Current approach requires new Go code for each tool:
- `cmd/bd/setup/cursor.go`
- `cmd/bd/setup/kilocode.go` (proposed)
- `cmd/bd/setup/windsurf.go` (future?)
- ...

This does not scale. All these integrations do the same thing:
1. Generate markdown content (beads workflow instructions)
2. Write to tool-specific path

The **content** is identical. Only the **destination path** varies.

## Design

### Core Insight

Beads does not need to know anything about Kilo Code, Cursor, or Claude Code beyond where they read their rules files. That is a path mapping—data, not code.

### Recipe Format

```toml
# .beads/recipes.toml (user additions/overrides)
[cursor]
path = ".cursor/rules/bd.md"

[claude]
path = "CLAUDE.md"

[kilocode]
path = ".kilocode/rules/bd.md"

[my-custom-editor]
path = ".myeditor/config/bd.md"
```

### Built-in Defaults

Ship sensible defaults compiled into binary:
- cursor → `.cursor/rules/bd.md`
- claude → `CLAUDE.md`
- windsurf → `.windsurf/rules/bd.md`
- cody → `.cody/rules/bd.md` (verify path)

User file overrides/extends built-in defaults.

### Commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `bd setup <recipe>` | Write template to recipe path |
| `bd setup --list` | Show available recipes (built-in + user) |
| `bd setup --print` | Dump template to stdout |
| `bd setup -o <path>` | Write to arbitrary path (escape hatch) |
| `bd setup <recipe> --check` | Verify if installed |
| `bd setup <recipe> --remove` | Delete the file |
| `bd setup --add <name> <path>` | Save custom recipe |

### Template

Single markdown template embedded in binary. Contains:
- What beads is
- Key commands (`bd ready`, `bd create`, `bd close`, etc.)
- Workflow guidance
- JSON flags for programmatic use

### Implementation Plan

1. Add `--print` and `-o` flags to existing setup command
2. Create `internal/recipes/` package
   - Built-in recipes (Go map or embed)
   - Load/merge user `.beads/recipes.toml`
3. Refactor `cmd/bd/setup/setup.go` to use recipes
4. Remove tool-specific files (`cursor.go`, etc.)
5. Add `--add` command to save custom recipes
6. Update documentation

### Migration

Existing `bd setup cursor` continues to work—just backed by recipe lookup instead of dedicated code.

## Out of Scope (for now)

- `--global` flag (user-level config paths) — use `-o` escape hatch
- `mode: append` (add to existing file) — wait until requested
- GitHub recipe registry — overkill for path mappings
- TOML migration for config.yaml — separate initiative

## Response to GH#898

Once implemented, respond to PR:

> Thanks for the contribution! We are refactoring `bd setup` to a recipe-based approach where new tools are config entries, not code changes.
>
> Instead of new Go files, please submit a PR adding Kilo Code to the built-in recipes in `internal/recipes/builtin.go` (or equivalent). Happy to help if you have questions!

## References

- GH#898: Kilo Code integration request
- Current setup code: `cmd/bd/setup/`
title: Refactor bd setup to recipe-based architecture (GH#898)

description:
dispatched_by: beads/crew/dave

## Problem

Beads has accumulated issue types that are specific to Gas Town's orchestration model:

```go
TypeAgent   IssueType = "agent"   // Agent identity bead
TypeRole    IssueType = "role"    // Agent role definition  
TypeRig     IssueType = "rig"     // Rig identity bead (project container)
TypeConvoy  IssueType = "convoy"  // Cross-project tracking
TypeSlot    IssueType = "slot"    // Exclusive access slot (merge-slot gate)
```

This violates separation of concerns. Beads is supposed to be a **generic AI-friendly issue tracker** that Gas Town happens to use - not "Gas Town's issue tracker."

## Impact

- **Credibility**: Beads can't be presented as a standalone tool when it has hardcoded knowledge of a specific orchestration system
- **Coupling**: Changes to Gas Town's identity model require beads releases
- **Confusion**: Users outside Gas Town see types that don't apply to them

## Solution

### Option A: Labels instead of types
Gas Town uses labels like `gt:agent`, `gt:role`, `gt:rig` instead of dedicated types. Beads stays generic.

### Option B: Type extension mechanism
Beads supports custom type registration via config. Gas Town registers its types at init time.

### Option C: Separate identity store
Gas Town maintains agent/role/rig identity outside beads entirely. Beads only tracks work items.

## Types to evaluate

| Type | Keep in beads? | Rationale |
|------|----------------|-----------|
| `gate` | Yes | Generic workflow concept (async waits) |
| `molecule` | Yes | Generic workflow templates |
| `agent` | **No** | Gas Town identity |
| `role` | **No** | Gas Town identity |
| `rig` | **No** | Gas Town identity |
| `convoy` | **No** | Gas Town cross-rig tracking |
| `slot` | Maybe | Could be generic "mutex" concept |

## Files affected

- `internal/types/types.go` - Type definitions
- `cmd/bd/create.go` - Type validation
- `cmd/bd/update.go` - Type validation  
- `internal/storage/sqlite/migrations/030_agent_fields.go` - Schema fields
- Possibly others with type switch statements
title: Extract Gas Town-specific issue types from beads core
Test actor flag

description:
In bondMolMol, the BondRef.ProtoID field is used to store molecule IDs:

```go
BondedFrom: append(molA.BondedFrom, types.BondRef{
    ProtoID:   molB.ID,  // This is a molecule, not a proto
    ...
})
```

This is semantically confusing since ProtoID suggests it should only hold proto references.

**Options:**
1. Rename ProtoID to SourceID (breaking change, needs migration)
2. Add documentation clarifying ProtoID can hold molecule IDs in bond context
3. Leave as-is, accept the naming is imprecise

Low priority since it's just naming, not functionality.
title: BondRef.ProtoID field name is misleading for mol+mol bonds

description:
Add --continue flag to bd close for seamless molecule step transitions.

## Usage

bd close <step-id> --continue [--no-auto]

## Behavior

1. Closes the specified step
2. Finds next ready step in same molecule (sibling/child)
3. By default, marks it in_progress (--no-auto to skip)
4. Outputs the transition

## Output

[done] Closed gt-abc.3: Implement feature

Next ready in molecule:
  gt-abc.4: Write tests

[arrow] Marked in_progress (use --no-auto to skip)

## If no next step

[done] Closed gt-abc.6: Exit decision

Molecule gt-abc complete! All steps closed.
Consider: bd mol squash gt-abc --summary '...'

## Key behaviors
- Detects parent molecule from closed step
- Finds next unblocked sibling
- Auto-claims by default (propulsion principle)
- Graceful handling when molecule is complete

## Gas Town integration
- gt-lz13: Update templates with nav workflow
- gt-um6q: Update docs with nav workflow
title: bd close --continue: auto-advance to next molecule step

description:
The 'CloseIssue', 'UpdateIssueID', and 'RemoveLabel' methods were fixed in PR #348 to prevent foreign key constraint failures when operating on non-existent issues.

However, the Oracle identified two other methods that follow the same problematic pattern:
1. `AddComment` (in `internal/storage/sqlite/events.go`)
2. `ApplyCompaction` (in `internal/storage/sqlite/compact.go`)

These methods attempt to insert an event record after updating the issue, without verifying that the issue update actually affected any rows. This leads to a foreign key constraint failure if the issue does not exist.

We need to:
1. Create reproduction tests for these failure cases
2. Apply the same fix pattern: check `RowsAffected()` after the update, and return a proper "issue not found" error if it is 0, before attempting to insert the event.
3. Standardize the error message format to "issue %s not found" or "issue not found: %s" for consistency.
title: Fix FOREIGN KEY constraint failures in AddComment and ApplyCompaction

description:
## Problem
`bd list` by default shows all non-closed issues including hooked ones. Hooked work is actively being worked on and shouldn't appear in the pool of available work.

## Solution
Add a `--ready` flag to `bd list` that filters to show only work that can actually be picked up:
- Status is `open` (not hooked, not in_progress, not blocked, not deferred)
- Could potentially also exclude assigned issues (optional consideration)

## Current Workaround
`bd list --status open` works but isn't as intuitive as `--ready`

## Implementation
- Add `--ready` boolean flag to list command
- When set, filter to status=open only
- Consider if it should also filter out assigned issues (probably not by default)
title: Add --ready flag to bd list to show only available work

description:
Currently bdt stores issues in JSONL format in issues.toon file. Phase 2.2 must implement actual TOON format storage - this is the fundamental goal of the bdtoon project.

## Current State (Phase 2.1)
- issues.toon stores JSONL (intermediate format)
- --toon flag allows output in TOON format for LLM consumption
- Problem: We're not actually using TOON as the fundamental storage format

## Required Work (Phase 2.2)
1. Switch issue file I/O to write TOON format instead of JSONL
   - Update cmd/bdt/storage.go to use EncodeTOON for writing
   - Update cmd/bdt/storage.go to decode TOON (currently decodes JSON)
   - Ensure round-trip: write TOON → read TOON → write TOON is byte-identical

2. Update command implementations
   - cmd/bdt/create.go: Write newly created issues to TOON format
   - cmd/bdt/list.go: Read issues from TOON format
   - cmd/bdt/show.go: Read from TOON format
   - cmd/bdt/import.go: Convert imported JSONL to TOON
   - cmd/bdt/export.go: Export TOON to JSONL (for bd compatibility)

3. Implement TOON parser that handles gotoon's encoder-only limitation
   - Since gotoon doesn't decode TOON, need custom TOON→JSON decoder
   - OR continue storing TOON but decoding via intermediate JSON conversion

4. Git merge driver optimization
   - TOON is line-oriented, better for 3-way merges than binary formats
   - Configure git merge driver for .toon files

5. Comprehensive testing
   - Round-trip tests: Issue → TOON → storage → read → Issue
   - Merge conflict resolution tests with TOON format
   - Large issue set performance tests

## Success Criteria
- issues.toon stores actual TOON format (not JSONL)
- bdt list reads from TOON file
- bdt create writes to TOON file
- Round-trip: create issue → list → show returns identical data
- All 65+ tests still passing
- Performance comparable to JSONL storage
title: Phase 2.2: Switch bdt storage to TOON format
